title.zh: 卷六十上 馬融列傳
title.raw: 卷六十上 馬融列傳 Volume 60a: Biography of Ma Rong

sentences.zh: 馬融字季長，扶風茂陵人也，[一]將作大匠嚴之子。

translations.literal: Ma Rong, courtesy name Jizhang, was a man of Maoling in Fufeng; [1] he was the son of Yan, the Grand Architect.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong, styled Jizhang, came from Maoling in Fufeng. He was the son of Yan, who served as Grand Architect of Works.

translations.literal: [2] In person he was handsome in speech and bearing, and possessed outstanding talent.
translations.idiomatic: He was gifted in both speech and looks, and carried uncommon intellectual brilliance.

sentences.zh: 初，京兆摯恂以儒術教授，隱於南山，不應徵聘，名重關西，[三]融從其遊學，博通經籍。

translations.literal: At first, Zhi Xun of Jingzhao taught Confucian learning, lived in seclusion on the Southern Mountain, did not respond to summons or appointments, and his name carried great weight west of the passes; [3] Rong followed him in study and travel, and thoroughly mastered the classics.
translations.idiomatic: Early on, Zhi Xun of Jingzhao taught the Confucian curriculum from seclusion on Mount Zhongnan, refusing court appointments; his reputation towered in the Guanxi region. Ma Rong studied under him on the road and gained wide mastery of the canonical texts.

translations.literal: Xun marveled at Rong's talent and gave him his daughter as wife.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Xun thought highly of Ma Rong's ability and married him to his daughter.

sentences.zh: 注[一]融集云：「茂陵成歡裡人也。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The collected works of Rong state: "He was a man of the Cheng Huan neighborhood in Maoling."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Ma Rong's own writings record that he was from the Cheng Huan ward of Maoling.

translations.literal: Note [2] Yan was the son of Yu, the elder brother of Ma Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Yan was the son of Yu, elder brother to the general Ma Yuan.

sentences.zh: 注[三]三輔決錄注曰：「恂字季直，好學善屬文，隱於南山之陰。」

translations.literal: Note [3] The commentary to the Sanfu Juelu says: "Xun's courtesy name was Jizhi; he loved learning and was skilled at composition; he lived in seclusion on the northern slope of the Southern Mountain."
translations.idiomatic: The Sanfu Juelu adds that Zhi Xun, styled Jizhi, loved scholarship and excelled at writing, and dwelt in retirement on the shaded northern face of the Southern Mountain.

sentences.zh: 永初二年，大將軍鄧騭聞融名，召為舍人，非其好也，遂不應命，客於涼州武都、漢陽界中。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongchu, the Grand General Deng Zhi heard Rong's name and summoned him as a retainer-clerk; it was not to his liking, so he did not answer the summons and lodged as a guest on the border between Wudu and Hanyang in Liangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: In 108 CE, Grand General Deng Zhi, having heard Ma Rong's reputation, offered him a post as palace gentleman—but that life did not suit him. He declined and wandered as a guest between Wudu and Hanyang in Liang province.

sentences.zh: 會羌虜坎起，邊方擾亂，米谷踴貴，自關以西，道殣相望。

translations.literal: It happened that Qiang raiders burst forth; the border regions were thrown into turmoil; rice and grain soared in price; west of the passes, corpses of the starved lined the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Then Qiang rebels erupted along the frontier; grain prices skyrocketed, and west of the passes the dead from famine littered the highways—a sight repeated mile after mile.

sentences.zh: [一] 融既饑困，乃悔而歎息，謂其友人曰：「古人有言：『左手據天下之圖，右手刎其喉，愚夫不為。

translations.literal: [1] When Rong was hungry and in distress, he repented and sighed, and said to his friend: "The ancients had a saying: 'With the left hand grasping the map of the empire, with the right hand cutting one's throat—a fool would not do it.'
translations.idiomatic: Pressed by hunger and want, Ma Rong reconsidered and sighed to a companion: "The old proverb runs: grasp the realm's map in your left hand and a blade to your throat in your right—even a dull man would refuse that bargain."

translations.literal: '[2] The reason is that life is more precious than the empire."
translations.idiomatic: The point, as the gloss adds, is that one's life outweighs dominion over the world."

sentences.zh: 今以曲俗咫尺之羞，滅無貲之軀，殆非老莊所謂也。

translations.literal: Now, for the shame of vulgar custom within a foot's reach, to destroy a body without resources—hardly what Laozi and Zhuangzi meant."
translations.idiomatic: To let petty shame at vulgar opinion within arm's length cost me a life I cannot afford—that is scarcely the teaching of Laozi and Zhuangzi."

translations.literal: " Therefore he went to answer Zhi's summons.
translations.idiomatic: So he went and accepted Deng Zhi's invitation.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳曰，叔向云：「道殣相望。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Zuo Zhuan says, Shu Xiang said: "Corpses of the starved lined the roads.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary cites the Zuo Zhuan, where Shu Xiang speaks of famine victims dead along the road, one after another.

translations.literal: " Du's commentary says, "To starve to death is jin."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses jin as dying of starvation.

translations.literal: Pronunciation jin.
translations.idiomatic: Read jin (same as in 饉 'famine').

translations.literal: Note [2] The Zhuangzi says.
translations.idiomatic: The second note points to the Zhuangzi.

translations.literal: It speaks of not harming one's life for the sake of fame.
translations.idiomatic: It warns against trading life for reputation.

sentences.zh: 四年，拜為校書郎中，[一]詣東觀典校秘書。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, he was appointed collator in the Bureau of Attendants; [1] he went to the Eastern Lodge to collate the secret archives.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year of Yongchu he received appointment as collating gentleman and was sent to the Eastern Institute to oversee the palace library.

sentences.zh: 是時鄧太后臨朝，騭兄弟輔政。

translations.literal: At this time Empress Dowager Deng held court, and Zhi's brothers assisted in government.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Deng was regent; Deng Zhi and his brothers guided the administration.

sentences.zh: 而俗儒世士，以為文德可興，武功宜廢，遂寢搜狩之禮，息戰陳之法，故猾賊從橫，乘此無備。

translations.literal: Yet vulgar Ru scholars and gentlemen of the age held that civil virtue could be promoted and military accomplishment ought to be discarded; they therefore let fall the rites of the grand hunts and ceased the methods of battle formations—so wily bandits ran rampant and exploited this lack of readiness.
translations.idiomatic: Pedantic scholars argued that culture should flourish while arms were laid aside; they abandoned the royal hunt and drill, leaving the realm unprepared—so rebels and raiders flourished in the opening.

sentences.zh: 融乃感激，以為文武之道，聖賢不墜，五才之用，無或可廢。

translations.literal: Rong was stirred and held that the way of wen and wu was not cast aside by sages and worthies, and among the uses of the five materials none might properly be abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong was moved to argue that sage kings never renounced both civil and martial arts, and that of the five elements none—not even arms—could responsibly be discarded.

sentences.zh: [二]元初二年，上廣成頌以諷諫。

translations.literal: [2] In the second year of Yuanchu, he submitted the "Rhapsody on Guangcheng" to remonstrate indirectly.
translations.idiomatic: In 115 CE he presented his "Guangcheng Rhapsody" as tactful admonition.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：[三] 注[一]謝承*[書]*及續漢書並雲為校書郎，又拜郎中也。

translations.literal: Its words say: [3] Note [1] Xie Cheng's Book and the Xu Han shu both say he was collating gentleman and was also appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: The piece begins—note that Xie Cheng and the Continuation of the Han History both record the post as collator, then appointment as a palace gentleman.

sentences.zh: 注[二]五才，金、木、水、火、土也。

translations.literal: Note [2] The five materials are metal, wood, water, fire, and earth.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the "five materials" are the traditional five phases—metal, wood, water, fire, and earth.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰，宋子罕曰「天生五材，人並用之，廢一不可，誰能去兵」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan says, Song Zihan said: "Heaven produced the five materials, and people use them all; to discard one is impossible—who can do away with weapons?"
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan quotes Song Zihan: Heaven gave five resources for human use; none may be cast aside—least of all arms.

sentences.zh: 注[三]廣成，苑，在今汝州梁縣西。

translations.literal: Note [3] Guangcheng is a park, west of Liang County in modern Ruzhou.
translations.idiomatic: The Guangcheng imperial park lay west of Liang county in present-day Ruzhou, Henan.

sentences.zh: 臣聞孔子曰：「奢則不遜，儉則固。

translations.literal: I have heard Confucius say: "If extravagant, one is not deferential; if frugal, one is rustic."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius taught that excess breeds arrogance, while too much austerity leaves one crude.

translations.literal: " Between extravagance and frugality, take ritual as the boundary.
translations.idiomatic: Between those poles, the rites mark the proper limit.

sentences.zh: [一]是以蟋蟀、山樞之人，並刺國君，諷以太康馳驅之節。

translations.literal: [1] Therefore the men of "Cricket" and "The Mountain Storax" both satirize the lords of their states, admonishing with the measure of not being too at ease in driving and galloping.
translations.idiomatic: The odes "Cricket" and "The Mountain Elms" each rebuke a duke, warning against the excess of untrammeled ease and sport.

sentences.zh: [二]夫樂而不荒，憂而不困，[三]先王所以平和府藏，頤養精神，致之無疆。

translations.literal: [2] To take joy and yet not be dissolute, to grieve and yet not be overwhelmed—[3] this is how former kings balanced the storehouses, nourished the spirit, and reached boundless peace.
translations.idiomatic: Delight without dissipation, care without despair—that is how the ancient kings harmonized the treasuries, refreshed the mind, and secured lasting order.

translations.literal: [4] Therefore the striking of the yu and the chiming of the jade qiu are recorded in the Counsels of Yu;
translations.idiomatic: Hence the Shang shu’s "Counsels of Yu" memorializes the strike of mallets and the ring of jade chimes.

translations.literal: "Auspicious Day" and "Chariot Assault" are ordered in the Zhou odes.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Odes places the hunt pieces "Auspicious Day" and "Our Chariots, Strong" in the Canon of Zhou.

sentences.zh: [五]聖主賢君，以增盛美，豈徒為奢淫而已哉！

translations.literal: [5] Holy lords and worthy kings used these to add to their splendor—how could it be merely for wanton luxury!
translations.idiomatic: Sage sovereigns employed such pageantry to crown their reigns, not to indulge mere excess.

sentences.zh: 伏見元年已來，遭值□運，[六]陛下戒懼災異，躬自菲薄，荒□禁苑，廢□樂懸，勤憂潛思，十有餘年，以過禮數。

translations.literal: I humbly observe that since the first year, having encountered ill-omened fate, [6] Your Majesty has been wary of portents, personally abased himself, let the forbidden park go wild, set aside the suspended music, toiled in anxious thought for more than ten years, going beyond the measure of ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Since your accession, Heaven’s portents have kept you vigilant: you have denied yourself, let the royal parks run fallow, silenced the court instruments, and brooded in care for over a decade—an austerity that outstrips the rites’ demand.

sentences.zh: 重以皇太后體唐堯親九族篤睦之德，陛下履有虞烝烝之孝，外捨諸家，每有憂疾，聖恩普勞，遣使交錯，稀有曠絕。

translations.literal: Added to this, the Empress Dowager embodies the virtue of Yao in loving the nine kindreds; Your Majesty practices the filial xu xu of Shun, setting aside the various houses; whenever there is worry or illness, sacred grace everywhere labors, envoys go back and forth, rarely is there long interruption.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager, like Yao, cherishes every branch of the clan; you, like Shun, pour out filial devotion, slighting your own kin. At each illness or anxiety you dispatch messengers without long pause—your solicitude never flags.

sentences.zh: 時時寧息，又無以自娛樂，殆非所以逢迎太和，裨助萬福也。

translations.literal: Yet in the moments of quiet there is nothing wherewith to entertain oneself—hardly the way to welcome great harmony or assist the myriad blessings.
translations.idiomatic: Yet in rare calm you have no outlet for refreshment—hardly a way to court cosmic concord or secure every blessing.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為雖尚頗有蝗蟲，今年五月以來，雨露時澍，祥應將至。

translations.literal: In my foolish view, although there are still locusts, since the fifth month of this year rain and dew have fallen in season, and auspicious responses are about to arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Locusts may linger, but since the fifth month timely rains have fallen; good omens will follow.

sentences.zh: 方涉冬節，農事閒隙，宜幸廣成，覽原隰，觀宿麥，*[勸]*收藏，因講武校獵，使寮庶百姓，復鶯羽旄之美，聞鐘鼓之音，歡嬉喜樂，鼓舞疆畔，[七]以迎和氣，招致休慶。

translations.literal: As we enter the winter season and farm work has a gap, it is fitting to visit Guangcheng, survey the highlands and lowlands, observe the winter wheat, encourage storage and gathering, and thereupon discuss arms and hold a hunt review, so that officials and common people again see the beauty of oriole-feather banners and hear the sound of bells and drums, rejoice and delight, dance along the fields’ edges, [7] to welcome harmonious qi and summon felicitous celebration.
translations.idiomatic: Winter brings a lull in the fields: this is the season to progress to Guangcheng, tour wet and dry ground, inspect the winter wheat, urge the harvest in, and hold maneuvers and a royal hunt. Let court and country again see plumes and streamers, hear drums and bells, and take heart along the borderlands—inviting gentle weather and heaping blessing.

translations.literal: Your petty subject, an ant, cannot bear the trifling.
translations.idiomatic: I am but a minor official, no more than an ant, yet I cannot hold back this humble plea.

sentences.zh: 職在書籍，謹依舊文，重述搜狩之義，作頌一篇，並封上。

translations.literal: My duty lies in the books; respectfully following the old texts, I have restated the meaning of the grand hunt and composed a eulogistic rhapsody, which I enclose and submit.
translations.idiomatic: My office tends the archives; I have therefore followed classical precedent, restated the meaning of the royal hunt, and submit a rhapsody under seal.

translations.literal: Shallow, mean, and thin—it is not worth perusing.
translations.idiomatic: The piece is shallow and unworthy of your attention.

translations.literal: Note [1] Jie is like xian 'limit.'
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: jie here means a fixed limit.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩國風序曰：「蟋蟀，刺晉僖公也。

translations.literal: Note [2] The preface to the Guo feng odes says: "Cricket satirizes Duke Xi of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Preface to the Airs of the States says "Cricket" rebukes Duke Xi of Jin.

translations.literal: He was frugal and did not hit the mark of ritual."
translations.idiomatic: His frugality failed to meet the measure of the rites."

translations.literal: " Its poem says: "Do not be too greatly at ease; attend to your duties in your place.
translations.idiomatic: The ode reads: "Cease your lordly ease—mind the duties at home."

translations.literal: " Mao Chang's commentary says: "Yi means 'excessive.'
translations.idiomatic: Mao Heng glosses yi as 'excess.'

sentences.zh: 」鄭箋云：「君雖當自樂，亦無甚太樂，欲其用禮以為節也。

translations.literal: " Zheng's subcommentary says: "Although the lord should take joy in himself, he should not be too greatly at ease; it desires that he use ritual as measure."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan adds: a ruler may enjoy himself, but not to dissipation; ritual must set the bounds.

translations.literal: " Again the preface says: "The Mountain Storax satirizes Duke Zhao of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: The Preface also says "The Mountain Elm" reproves Duke Zhao of Jin.

translations.literal: He had talent but could not use it."
translations.idiomatic: He possessed talent but wasted it."

translations.literal: " Its poem says: "You have chariots and horses, yet you do not gallop or drive.
translations.idiomatic: The lines run: "You own teams and cars yet never drive them out."

translations.literal: When you are dead indeed, others will take the pleasure."
translations.idiomatic: When you are gone, others will take your joy."

sentences.zh: 」言僖公以太康貽戒，昭公以不能馳驅被譏，言文武之道須折衷也。

translations.literal: " It speaks of Duke Xi being warned by too great ease, and Duke Zhao being ridiculed for inability to gallop and drive—it speaks of the way of wen and wu needing the middle way.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Xi is warned against idle ease, Zhao mocked for never riding forth—the moral is that civil and martial virtue demand balance.

translations.literal: Shu is pronounced ou.
translations.idiomatic: Read shu as ou (the tree name).

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳曰，吳季札聘於魯，魯為之歌頌。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Zuo Zhuan says: Wu Jizha was entertained in Lu, and Lu performed for him the hymns.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan records Prince Jizha of Wu visiting Lu, where he heard the Hymns performed.

translations.literal: Jizha said: "Joyful yet not dissolute.
translations.idiomatic: Jizha judged them "joyful yet never reckless."

translations.literal: " For him they sang Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Next they played the airs of Wei.

translations.literal: He said: "Sorrowful yet not distressed."
translations.idiomatic: He described them as "weighty yet never crushed."

sentences.zh: 注[四]韓詩外傳曰：「人有五藏六府。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Han shi waizhuan says: "People have five zang and six fu.
translations.idiomatic: Han Ying’s Outer Commentary asks: "Humankind stores spirit in five viscera and six chambers."

translations.literal: What are called the five zang?
translations.idiomatic: What counts as the five viscera?

sentences.zh: 精藏於腎，神藏於心，魂藏於肝，魄藏於肺，志藏於脾，此之謂五藏也。

translations.literal: Essence is stored in the kidneys, spirit in the heart, hun in the liver, po in the lungs, will in the spleen—these are called the five zang.
translations.idiomatic: Essence lodges in the kidneys, spirit in the heart, the ethereal soul in the liver, the corporeal soul in the lungs, and intent in the spleen—those are the five stores.

translations.literal: What are called the six fu?
translations.idiomatic: And the six 'palaces'?

translations.literal: The throat is the fu that measures the intestines;
translations.idiomatic: The gullet is the yardstick of the bowels.

translations.literal: The stomach is the fu of the five grains;
translations.idiomatic: The stomach is the granary of the five crops.

translations.literal: The large intestine is the fu of conveyance;
translations.idiomatic: The large intestine is the transport depot.

translations.literal: The small intestine is the fu that receives completion;
translations.idiomatic: The small intestine completes digestion.

translations.literal: The gall is the fu that accumulates essence;
translations.idiomatic: The gall bladder stockpiles refined essences.

translations.literal: The bladder is the fu that gathers fluids."
translations.idiomatic: The bladder collects the humors."

translations.literal: " The poem says: "Heaven birthed the teeming people, and for each thing there is a pattern."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic adds: "Heaven overspread the mass of folk, each thing with its proper rule."

translations.literal: Note [5] Jia is yu, pronunciation gu-ba fan.
translations.idiomatic: Jia denotes the yu scraper, read with the old fanqie spelling gu-ba.

sentences.zh: 形如伏獸，背上有二十七刻，以木長尺櫟之，所以止樂。

translations.literal: Its form is like a crouching beast; on its back are twenty-seven notches; with a wooden stick a foot long one strikes it—thereby stopping the music.
translations.idiomatic: Shaped like a crouching animal with twenty-seven teeth, it is struck with a one-foot mallet to cut off the music.

sentences.zh: 擊，柷也，像桶，中有椎柄，連底搖之，所以作樂。

translations.literal: Ji is zhu; it resembles a bucket; inside is a mallet handle; connected to the bottom, one shakes it—thereby beginning the music.
translations.idiomatic: The zhu looks like a tub with an inner pestle on a swivel—shake it to begin the piece.

translations.literal: See the Illustrations of the Three Rituals.
translations.idiomatic: See the Three Rituals diagrams.

translations.literal: Qiu is the jade qing.
translations.idiomatic: Qiu is the jade chime-stone.

translations.literal: The Counsels of Yu is the Canon of Shun.
translations.idiomatic: The "Counsels of Yu" section belongs to the Canon of Shun.

sentences.zh: 詩小雅曰：「吉日維戊，既伯既禱。

translations.literal: The Minor Odes of the Book of Poetry say: "The auspicious day is wu; we have made the bo offering and prayed.
translations.idiomatic: The Lesser Ya intones: "This lucky day, the fifth stem—we have offered to the herd-lord and prayed."

translations.literal: The field chariots are already fine; the four stallions are very tall."
translations.idiomatic: Our hunting cars are trim; the four bays tower high."

translations.literal: " It also says: "Our chariots are strong; our horses are well matched."
translations.idiomatic: Elsewhere: "Our chariots are tight; our teams move as one."

sentences.zh: 注[六]元年謂安帝即位年也。

translations.literal: Note [6] The first year means the year of Emperor An's accession.
translations.idiomatic: The commentator takes "first year" as the year Emperor An took the throne (107 CE).

translations.literal: The ill-omened fate means things like earthquakes, great floods, and hail.
translations.idiomatic: The missing graph marks Heaven’s ill favor—earthquakes, floods, hailstorms, and the like.

sentences.zh: 注[七]孟子對齊宣王曰：「今王*

translations.literal: Note [7] Mencius said to King Xuan of Qi: "If Your Majesty now
translations.idiomatic: Mencius once told King Xuan of Qi: "If you, my king,

translations.literal: (po 'rather')
translations.idiomatic: (Text variant: po 'quite.')

sentences.zh: *鼓樂於此，百姓聞王鐘鼓之聲，舉欣欣然有喜色而相告曰：『吾王庶幾無疾病歟？

translations.literal: have drums and music here, and the hundred clans hear Your Majesty's bells and drums; they all happily show joyful faces and tell one another: 'Our king perhaps has no illness?
translations.idiomatic: If you raise music here, the people hear your bells and drums, brighten with joy, and say to one another, ‘Perhaps our king is well?

translations.literal: How can he have drums and music!'
translations.idiomatic: How else could he make such music!’

sentences.zh: 』今王田獵於此，百姓見羽旄之美，欣欣有喜色而相告曰：『吾王庶幾無疾病歟？

translations.literal: ' Now Your Majesty hunts here, and the hundred clans see the beauty of plumes and pennons; happily they show joyful faces and tell one another: 'Our king perhaps has no illness?
translations.idiomatic: ’ When you hunt here and folk see the splendor of your banners, they smile and ask the same: ‘Is our king hale?

translations.literal: How can he hunt!'
translations.idiomatic: How else could he ride to the chase!’

translations.literal: ' This is nothing else but sharing joy with the people."
translations.idiomatic: ’ There is no other secret: you share their pleasures."

sentences.zh: 臣聞昔命師於鞬櫜，偃伯於靈台，或人嘉而稱焉。

translations.literal: I have heard that of old the army was ordered at the bow-case and scabbard, and Bo was laid to rest at Lingtai—some men praised and acclaimed this.
translations.idiomatic: They say the ancient kings summoned armies from the quiver and silenced war-horses at Lingtai; romantics still praise that restraint.

sentences.zh: [一]彼固未識夫雷霆之為天常，金革之作昏明也。

translations.literal: [1] They surely did not recognize that thunderclaps are Heaven's constant, and that the making of metal and leather marks dusk and dawn.
translations.idiomatic: Those critics never grasped that thunder belongs to Heaven’s regular pulse, or that arms and armor measure night from day.

translations.literal: [2] Before Huang and Yan, the transmitted way was unrecorded;
translations.idiomatic: Before the Yellow Emperor and Yan Di, tradition hardly survives.

translations.literal: since the Three and Five, one may hear something in outline.
translations.idiomatic: From the Three August Ones and Five Emperors onward we catch only faint echoes.

sentences.zh: 且區區之酆郊，猶廓七十里之囿，盛春秋之苗。

translations.literal: Moreover, the mere Feng suburb still opened a seventy-li park, flourishing the spring and autumn hunts.
translations.idiomatic: Even petty Feng kept a seventy-li royal park and staged spring and autumn hunts.

translations.literal: [3] The poetry sings of
translations.idiomatic: The Odes hymned

translations.literal: (you 'park')
translations.idiomatic: (Graph: you 'enclosed park.')

translations.literal: **[pu]* grass; music performed 'Zouyu.'
translations.idiomatic: The ode sings of parkland herbs while musicians played "Zouyu."

sentences.zh: [四]是以大漢之初基也，宅茲天邑，總風雨之會，交陰陽之和。

translations.literal: [4] Therefore at the founding of the great Han, dwelling in this celestial city, it gathered the meeting of wind and rain, mingled the harmony of yin and yang.
translations.idiomatic: Thus at Han’s founding the capital claimed Heaven’s mandate, drew wind and rain into one place, and wedded yin to yang.

translations.literal: [5] Surveying that spirit park, it set camp at the southern suburb.
translations.idiomatic: They measured the sacred hunting ground and laid out its lodges south of the city.

sentences.zh: [六]徒觀其□場區宇，恢胎曠蕩，□敻勿罔，寥豁鬱泱，[七] 騁望千里，天與地莽。

translations.literal: [6] Merely beholding its parade grounds (one graph missing in the source), vast and rolling, endlessly remote and without limit, open wide and luxuriantly immense, [7] one lets the eye gallop a thousand li—Heaven and earth merge into scrubland.
translations.idiomatic: Look across the parade ground where the manuscript is damaged: horizons swell, misted distances blur, and the eye races a thousand li until sky and scrubland merge.

sentences.zh: 於是周阹環瀆，右矕三塗，左概嵩岳，[八]面據衡陰，箕背王屋，浸以波、溠，夤以滎、洛。

translations.literal: Thereupon encircling drives and ring ditches; to the right overlooking San Tu; to the left embracing Song Mountain; [8] facing it grips the north of Heng; on its back is Wangwu; soaked by the Bo and Zha; linked by the Ying and Luo.
translations.idiomatic: Round about run deer-fences and moats: San Tu rises to the right, Mount Song anchors the left. Ahead lies the northern flank of Mount Heng; behind tower the Wangwu ridges. The Bo and Zha rivers soak its skirts; the Ying and Luo wind along its flanks.

sentences.zh: [九]金山、石林，殷起乎其中，峨峨磑磑，鏘鏘□□，隆穹盤回，嵎峗錯崔。

translations.literal: [9] Gold mountains and stone forests rise massively therein, lofty and steep, clang-clang (two graphs illegible in the received text), lofty domes winding and turning, rugged crags interlocking sheer.
translations.idiomatic: Gold-hued ridges and stone forests surge upward—towering, jagged, with a ringing resonance where the manuscript is damaged—domed peaks coil while broken cliffs crowd in dizzy tiers.

sentences.zh: [一０]神泉側出，丹水涅池，怪石浮磬，耀焜於其陂。

translations.literal: [10] Divine springs gush from the side; cinnabar streams and the Nie pools; strange rocks like floating chime-stones, blazing with light on their banks.
translations.idiomatic: [10] Sacred springs well from the flanks, cinnabar-tinted waters and dark pools thread between, and fantastical boulders like suspended lithophones flash along the levees.

sentences.zh: [一一]其土毛則搉牧薦草，芳茹甘荼，[一二]茈萁、芸蒩，昌本、深蒱，[一三]芝荋、菫、荁，蘘荷、芋渠，[一四]桂荏、鳧葵，格、□、菹、於。

translations.literal: [11] Its native plants include quo pasture fodder-thistle and offerings of marsh-grass, fragrant roots and sweet bitterleaf; [12] purple fern-bracken, rutaceous weed and pickerelweed, iris-root and deep-water cattail; [13] fungus-cap herbs, violet-rabbit-ear and hog-peanut, myoga ginger and taro-yam; [14] perilla and mallard-flower, gelidium (one graph missing in text), pickle-mallow and yu.
translations.idiomatic: [11] The turf yields hay for sacrifice and sweet meadow fodder, scented roots and mellow bitter greens; [12] royal fern and fragrant weeds, irises and pond-reeds; [13] wild mushrooms, violets, and legumes, myoga and taro; [14] perilla, water-lilies, and more whose names the damaged text only half records.

sentences.zh: [一五]其植物則玄林包竹，藩陵蔽京，珍林嘉樹，建木叢生，[一六]椿、梧、栝、柏，櫃、柳、楓、楊，[一七]豐彤對蔚，崟蹌槮爽。

translations.literal: [15] Its vegetation includes dark forests wrapping bamboo, hedge hills shading the high bank, treasured woods and fine trees, with cosmic pillar trees clustering thickly; [16] ailanthus, paulownia, juniper, cypress, chinquapin, willow, maple, and poplar; [17] abundant forms paired in luxuriance, lofty ranks towering sheer.
translations.idiomatic: [15] Ink-black groves swallow bamboo groves; ridges veil the capital mound while prized timber rises in thickets like the mythic Jian wood; [16] ailanthus, paulownia, hardy junipers and cypresses, oak, willow, maple, and poplar stand [17] in paired splendor, layered green ramparts scraping the sky.

sentences.zh: [一八]翕習春風，含津吐榮，舖於布□，蓶扈蘳熒，惡可殫形。

translations.literal: [18] They drink in the spring wind, hold sap and put forth glory, spread across (one graph missing); lush and gleaming, sparkling bright—how could one exhaust their forms?
translations.idiomatic: [18] They sip the spring breeze, swell with sap, and burst into bloom across slopes the manuscript only partly names—too lush, too luminous to describe in full.

sentences.zh: [一九] 注[一]鞬以藏箭，櫜以藏弓。

translations.literal: [19] Note [1] The jian holds arrows; the gao holds the bow.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the quiver case stores shafts; the bow-sheath cradles the bow.

translations.literal: Jian is read ji-yan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Pronounce jian with the fanqie ji-yan.

translations.literal: Gao is pronounced gao.
translations.idiomatic: Gao rhymes with ‘high’ (gao).

sentences.zh: 禮記孔子曰：「武王克殷，倒載干戈，包以獸皮，名之曰建櫜。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites: Confucius said: "When King Wu conquered Yin, he inverted spear and halberd, wrapped them in beast hide, and named it jian gao.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites quotes Confucius: after conquering Shang, King Wu bundled spear and dagger-axe in hides and called the bundle the ‘sealed bow-case.’

sentences.zh: 」鄭注云「建讀為鍵」，音其蹇反，謂藏閉之也，此馬鄭異義。

translations.literal: " Zheng's commentary says "jian is read as jian (bolt)", pronunciation qi-jian fan, meaning to store and close—this is where Ma and Zheng differ.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses jian as ‘bolt’ (read qi-jian fan), meaning to seal away—here the Ma and Zheng schools diverge.

sentences.zh: 司馬法曰：「古者武軍三年不興，則凱樂凱歌，偃伯靈台，荅人之勞，告不興也。

translations.literal: The Sima fa says: "In antiquity, if the martial host did not mobilize for three years, there was victory music and victory song; Bo was laid to rest at Lingtai, answering the people's toil, announcing that it would not rise."
translations.idiomatic: The Methods of the Marshal says that when three years passed without war, the army sang paeans and sheathed the commander’s baton at Lingtai, thanking the realm and declaring peace.

translations.literal: " Yan means rest.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Lay aside’ here means to rest.

translations.literal: Bo refers to the commander's baton.
translations.idiomatic: Bo is the marshal’s staff of authority.

translations.literal: Lingtai is the terrace for observing qi.
translations.idiomatic: Lingtai was the observation terrace for reading Heaven’s omens.

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳鄭子太叔曰：「為刑罰威獄，以類天之震耀殺戮。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Zuo Zhuan: Zi Taishu of Zheng said: "Make punishments and awe-inspiring prisons to resemble Heaven's thunder-flash killing and slaughter.
translations.idiomatic: Zi Taishu of Zheng explains that penal awe mimics Heaven’s thunderbolt slaughter.

translations.literal: " Du's commentary says: "Thunderclaps flashing—the awe of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: lightning-flash is Heaven’s majesty.

translations.literal: The sage instituted prisons to imitate them."
translations.idiomatic: So sages fashioned prisons in imitation.

sentences.zh: 」又宋子罕曰：「兵之設久矣，所以威不軌而昭文德也。

translations.literal: " Further, Song Zihan said: "The establishment of arms is long-standing—it is by this that irregular conduct is awed and civil virtue displayed.
translations.idiomatic: Song Zihan adds that armies exist to awe rebellion and clarify kingly virtue.

sentences.zh: 聖人以興，亂人以廢，廢興存亡昏明之術，皆兵之由也。」

translations.literal: By them sages rise; by them troublemakers fall—methods of decline and rise, survival and loss, darkness and light all come from arms."
translations.idiomatic: Sages rise and miscreants fall through them: war governs every turn from light to dark."

translations.literal: Note [3] Feng was where King Wen of Zhou established his capital.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Feng was King Wen’s Zhou capital.

sentences.zh: 孟子曰：「文王之囿方七十里。

translations.literal: Mencius said: "King Wen's park was seventy li square."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius records King Wen’s hunting park as seventy li on a side.

sentences.zh: 」爾雅曰：「春獵為搜，夏曰苗，秋曰獮，冬曰狩。」

translations.idiomatic: The Er ya defines the seasonal hunts: spring sou, summer seedling-round, autumn autumn-drive, winter winter hunt."

translations.literal: " The Mao edition says: "Those lush reeds—one shot brings down five piglets—ah, the Zouyu!
translations.idiomatic: The Mao text praises the marsh-reeds and the five boars dropped at one shot—“oh, the auspicious Zouyu beast!”

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「騶虞，義獸也，白虎黑文，不食生物。

translations.literal: " Mao Chang's commentary says: "Zouyu is a righteous beast, white tiger with black markings; it does not eat living creatures.
translations.idiomatic: Mao Heng identifies Zouyu as a righteous prodigy—a white tiger striped in black that takes no living prey.

translations.literal: When there is virtue of utmost good faith, it responds."
translations.idiomatic: It appears only when perfect trust rules the age."

sentences.zh: 」周禮大司樂：「王大射則奏騶虞。」

translations.idiomatic: The Rites of Zhou direct the court musicians to play “Zouyu” at the king’s great archery rite."

sentences.zh: 注[五]周禮曰：「風雨之所會也，陰陽之所和也，乃建王國焉。

translations.idiomatic: The Zhou li says the capital sits where winds and rains converge and yin and yang balance.

translations.literal: " The celestial city refers to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Heaven’s city’ here means Luoyang.

translations.idiomatic: Kui means to survey or lay out.

translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong means Guangcheng is modeled on that royal preserve.

translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu: corrals that follow ravines to trap game are called qu.

translations.idiomatic: Mount San Tu lies southwest of Luhun county.

translations.idiomatic: ‘North of Heng’ means the northern flank of Mount Heng.

translations.idiomatic: The Han geography monograph places Mount Zhi in Zhi county as the Li’s spring.

translations.idiomatic: That lies north of Xiangcheng in modern Nanyang prefecture.

translations.idiomatic: The Rites of Zhou list Bo and Zha as Yu province’s marshes, Ying and Luo as its main streams.

translations.literal: " The Shuijing zhu says "The Zha River comes forth from Huang Mountain."
translations.idiomatic: The Commentary on the Water Classic traces the Zha to Huang Mountain.

translations.idiomatic: That source lies northeast of Zaoyang in Suizhou.

translations.literal: It also says "The Bo River comes forth from Horse-Rest Ridge"—that is what Ying Shao calls solitary mountain whence Bo water issues.
translations.idiomatic: The same work sets the Bo at Horse-Rest Ridge—Ying Shao’s ‘lonely peak’ whence the Bo rises.

translations.literal: It is northwest of Lushan in modern Ruzhou.
translations.idiomatic: That spring lies northwest of Lushan in Ruzhou.

translations.idiomatic: Commentary: ‘Gold Mountain’ is Mount Golden Gate.

translations.idiomatic: Li Daoyuan places it south of Mianchi.

translations.idiomatic: The Luoyang county gazetteer describes a giant stone mass south of the city, wooded and capped by the Great Stone Shrine, two hundred zhang tall.

sentences.zh: 殷音於謹反，磑音五來反，□音徂回反，嵎音隅，峗音魚軌反，並高峻魍。

translations.idiomatic: Sound glosses: yin (yu-jin), wei (wu-lai), the damaged graph (cu-hui), yu as ‘corner,’ wei as yu-gui—all denote towering crags.

translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan quotes Milord Yu: ‘Everything sprung from this soil owes fealty—who is not subject or lord?’

translations.idiomatic: Commentary: xuan means somber or shadowed.

translations.literal: Bao means clustering growth.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Wrapping’ here means growing in thickets.

sentences.zh: 爾雅曰：「大阜曰陵，絕高曰京。

translations.literal: The Er ya says: "A great mound is called ling; surpassingly high is called jing.
translations.idiomatic: The Er ya defines a grand terrace as ling and a towering bank as jing.

translations.literal: " Fan also means to screen.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Hedge’ likewise means to veil.

translations.literal: Jian mu is tall timber.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Cosmic-axis trees’ are exceptionally lofty timber.

translations.literal: Note [17] They are all tree names.
translations.idiomatic: All entries in the list are names of trees.

translations.literal: Gui is pronounced ju.
translations.idiomatic: Read gui like ju.

translations.literal: Yang, damaged rime gloss reading yi-zheng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Yang: fanqie yi-zheng where the rime-group graph is missing.

translations.literal: Note [18] They are all appearances of forest trees.
translations.idiomatic: These binomes describe the look of forest stands.

translations.literal: Dui is read tu-dui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Dui: fanqie tu-dui.

translations.literal: Yin is pronounced yin.
translations.idiomatic: Yin rhymes with ‘chant.’

translations.literal: Shen is read suo-jin fan.
translations.idiomatic: Shen: fanqie suo-jin.

translations.literal: Shuang, damaged rime gloss reading sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang: read sheng per the damaged rime gloss.

translations.literal: Note [19] Pu is pronounced fu.
translations.idiomatic: Pu is read like fu ‘spread.’

translations.literal: Wei is read yi-kui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Wei: fanqie yi-kui.

sentences.zh: 郭璞注爾雅云：「草木花初出為髭。

translations.literal: Guo Pu's commentary on the Er ya says: "When plants first send forth flowers it is called zi.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu defines the first emergence of blossoms as zi.

sentences.zh: 」與蓶通，其字從「唯」，本作從「荏」者，誤也。

translations.literal: " It interchanges with wei; the character follows 'wei'; those originally written following 'ren' are erroneous.
translations.idiomatic: It parallels wei and should use the wei phonetic; copies with the ren radical are wrong.

translations.literal: Hu is pronounced hu.
translations.idiomatic: Hu as in ‘door.’

sentences.zh: 蘳音胡瓦反，字從「圭」，並花葉貌。

translations.literal: Gui is read hu-wa fan; the character follows 'gui'; both are appearances of flowers and leaves.
translations.idiomatic: Gui (fanqie hu-wa) takes the gui radical; both binomes paint foliage.

translations.literal: Some editions write
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts read

translations.literal: (gui)
translations.idiomatic: the graph gui.

translations.literal: where the text has a missing graph."
translations.idiomatic: That is where another graph stood.”

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Gui is yellow flower.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines gui as yellow blooms.

translations.literal: " The Guang ya says: "Fine color.
translations.idiomatic: The Guang ya glosses it as handsome coloration.

translations.literal: " Ying is light.
translations.idiomatic: Ying means gleaming light.

translations.literal: Wu means he 'what'; pronunciation wu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu is the interrogative ‘how,’ read wu.

sentences.zh: 至於陽月，陰慝害作，百草畢落，林衡戒田，焚萊柞木。

translations.literal: When it comes to the yang month, yin evil injury arises; the hundred plants all shed; the Forest Master warns the fields; they burn stubble and chop trees.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth month yin’s killing breath strikes; grasses brown; forest wardens ready the grounds—torching brush and lopping timber.

sentences.zh: [一]然後舉天網，頓八紘，揫斂九藪之動物，繯橐四野之飛征。

translations.literal: [1] Then they raise Heaven's net, gather the eight cords, collect the creatures of the nine marshes, and bag the soaring migrants of the four wilds.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Then Heaven’s net lifts, the eight reaches cinch tight, game from the nine marshlands is rounded up, and birds winging over the four wastes are netted in sacks.

sentences.zh: [二]鳩之乎茲囿之中，山敦雲移，腢鳴膠膠，鄙騃噪讙，子野聽聳，離朱目眩，隸首策亂，陳子籌昏。

translations.literal: [2] They gather them within this park—hill masses shifting like clouds; dove calls tangling; coarse beasts clamoring; Ziye strains to hear; Lizhu reels at sight; Lishou's tallies tangle; Chenzi's reckoning grows dim.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Creatures are driven into the enclosure—hillocks drift under racing clouds; turtle-doves coo in chorus; rough beasts bay; court musicians strain their ears; keen-eyed trackers grow dizzy; legendary reckoners lose count.

sentences.zh: [三]於時營圍恢廓，充斥川谷，罦罝羅羉，彌綸坑澤，□牢陵山。

translations.literal: [3] At this time the hunt-ring stretches vast, filling streams and valleys; fu nets, ju nets, luo nets, and lian nets span pits and marshes, (one graph missing) cage hills.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The battue swells until valleys brim—pheasant nets, hare snares, bird nets, and boar nets quilt pools and bogs and seal every ridge.

sentences.zh: [四]校隊案部，前後有屯，甲乙相伍，戊己為堅。

translations.literal: [4] The ranked squads array by section; front and rear have encampments; jia and yi pair off in fives; wu and ji stand firm as the core.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Companies fall into line—van and rear guard stand in camps, jia and yi files wheel together, while wu and ji anchor the center.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]爾雅曰：「十月為陽。

translations.literal: [5] Note [1] The Er ya says: "The tenth month is yang.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Commentary cites the Er ya: the tenth month is called yang.

sentences.zh: 」孫炎注曰：「純陰用事，嫌於無陽，故以名雲。」

translations.literal: " Sun Yan's commentary says: "Pure yin holds sway; fearing the absence of yang, therefore it is so named."
translations.idiomatic: Sun Yan explains that pure yin seems too bleak without yang, hence the name.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「唯正月之朔，慝未作。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan says: "Only on the new moon of the first month has evil not yet arisen.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan says evil does not stir until after the first month’s new moon.

translations.literal: " Du's commentary says: "Te is yin qi.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu defines te as yin’s breath.

sentences.zh: 害作言陰氣肅殺，害於百草也。

translations.literal: "Injury arising" speaks of yin qi's austere killing, injuring the hundred plants.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Harm arises’ means autumn’s killing frost scything every herb.

sentences.zh: 」周禮曰：「林衡掌巡林麓之禁令。

translations.literal: " The Zhou li says: "The Forest Master supervises patrolling the forest foot prohibitions.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites of Zhou charge Forest Masters with policing the woodland reserves.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「牧師掌牧地，凡田事贊焚萊。

translations.literal: " It also says: "The Pasture Master administers pasture lands; for all field affairs he assists in burning stubble.
translations.idiomatic: Pasture officials oversee grazing lands and help torch fallow before hunts.

translations.literal: " It removes grass.
translations.idiomatic: That is, clearing undergrowth.

translations.literal: Zha is read shi-ya fan; oblique chopping of trees.
translations.idiomatic: Zha (fanqie shi-ya) means angled timber-cutting.

sentences.zh: 周禮：「柞氏掌攻草木及林麓。」

translations.literal: Zhou li: "The Zha clan supervises attacking grass and trees down to the forest foot."
translations.idiomatic: The Rites assign the Zha officers to clear scrub and timber to the treeline.

translations.literal: Note [2] Qiu means gather; pronunciation zi-you fan.
translations.idiomatic: Qiu means ‘to gather,’ fanqie zi-you.

sentences.zh: 周禮職方氏掌九藪：楊州具區，荊州雲夢，豫州圃田，青州孟諸，兗州大野，雍州弦蒲，幽州貕養，冀州楊紆，并州昭余祁。

translations.literal: The Zhou li Director of Regions oversees the nine marshes: Yangzhou Juqu, Jingzhou Yunmeng, Yuzhou Putian, Qingzhou Mengzhu, Yanzhou Daye, Yongzhou Xianpu, Youzhou Xi yang, Jizhou Yangxu, Bingzhou Zhaoyuqi.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites list nine great marshlands—Tai Hu for Yang, Yunmeng for Jing, Park Field for Yu, Mengzhu for Qing, the Great Moor for Yan, Xianpu for Yong, Xi yang for You, Yangxu for Ji, Zhaoyuqi for Bing.

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "A marsh without open water is called sou.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan defines sou as seasonally dry marsh.

translations.literal: " Moving creatures means birds and beasts.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Moving creatures’ means furred and feathered game.

translations.literal: Huan is read hu-quan fan, also hu-chuan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Huan: alternate fanqie hu-quan or hu-chuan.

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Huan means snare.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen glosses huan as ‘drop-net.’

translations.literal: " The Guoyu says: "Ring-nets on mountains have han.
translations.idiomatic: The Discourses of the States speaks of mountain-ring nets strung with han birds.

translations.literal: " Jia Kui's commentary says: "Huan means encircle.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui reads huan as ‘encircle.’

translations.literal: " Tuo is a sack; pronunciation tuo.
translations.idiomatic: Tuo is a game bag, read tuo.

translations.literal: Four wilds: the wilds of the four directions.
translations.idiomatic: The ‘four wilds’ are the wastelands beyond the quarters.

translations.literal: Flying migrants: flying and running.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Flying migrants’ means birds on the wing and beasts on the run.

translations.literal: Note [3] Jiu means gather.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: jiu means to assemble.

translations.literal: Dun is pronounced tun; it also means pile up.
translations.idiomatic: Dun (read tun) also means ‘heap together.’

translations.literal: Bi ai is the appearance of beasts striving swiftly.
translations.idiomatic: Bi ai paints beasts lunging in frenzy.

translations.literal: Bi is read pu-mei fan; ai is read si.
translations.idiomatic: Bi: fanqie pu-mei; ai as in si ‘wait.’

sentences.zh: 韓詩曰：「駓駓俟俟，或腢或友。

translations.literal: The Han shi says: "Prancing, waiting, some paired, some friendly.
translations.idiomatic: Han Ying’s ode lines describe herds pairing off as mates or allies.

translations.literal: " Xuan means confusion, damaged rime gloss reading xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan means dazzled—read xuan per the damaged gloss.

translations.literal: Lishou was one skilled at reckoning in the time of the Yellow Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Lishou was the Yellow Emperor’s star accountant.

translations.literal: Chenzi is Chen Ping, skilled at stratagems.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Chenzi’ is Chen Ping, master of counters and schemes.

translations.literal: Hun means confusion.
translations.idiomatic: Hun means mental fog.

translations.literal: It speaks of birds and beasts being too many to reckon.
translations.idiomatic: The point is game beyond counting.

translations.literal: Note [4] Fu is pronounced fu; pheasant net.
translations.idiomatic: Fu (read fu) is the long net for pheasants.

translations.literal: Ju is the hare snare; the closing character of the compound is missing in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Ju is the hare snare (text breaks mid-word).

translations.literal: Lian is pig net; pronunciation li-guan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Lian nets swine—fanqie li-guan.

translations.literal: All appear in the Er ya.
translations.idiomatic: All are defined in the Er ya.

translations.literal: Keng is read ku-geng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Keng: fanqie ku-geng.

translations.literal: The Cangjie pian says: "Keng is ravine.
translations.idiomatic: The primer defines keng as a sunken ravine.

translations.literal: The first character of the binome is missing; the sense is like a pen or corral.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged binome means ‘to pen’ or trap.

sentences.zh: 孫卿子曰「□牢天下而制之，若制子孫」也。

translations.literal: Master Xun said the ruler should lock the realm as tight as a paddock and rule it like commanding sons and grandsons, though the manuscript lacks the opening graph.
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi speaks of boxing in the empire as easily as children—despite the lacuna.

translations.literal: Various editions have written lao zha—that is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Editions that read ‘stockade’ are corrupt.

sentences.zh: 注[五]周禮司馬職曰：「前後有屯。

translations.literal: Note [5] The Zhou li Director of Horses says: "Front and rear have encampments.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites of Zhou’s horse marshal prescribes van and rear camps.

translations.literal: " Jia and yi speak of sequence.
translations.idiomatic: Jia and yi mark successive ranks.

translations.literal: Wu means squad leader.
translations.idiomatic: Wu here denotes five-man squads with leaders.

translations.literal: Wu and ji stand in the center as the firm core.
translations.idiomatic: Wu and ji anchor the middle as the iron center.

sentences.zh: 乘輿乃以吉月之陽朔，登於疏鏤之金路，六驌獮之玄龍，建雄虹之旌夏，揭鳴鳶之修橦。

translations.literal: The imperial chariot, on the yang new moon of the auspicious month, mounts the openwork golden road-carriage, six suxian dapple greys (like) dark dragons, sets up a rainbow great banner, raises the kite-crying tall pennant-pole.
translations.idiomatic: On the tenth month’s lucky new moon the emperor mounts his pierced-bronze state carriage drawn by six flecked chargers like black dragons; rainbow banners rise and long hawk pennants spear the sky.

sentences.zh: [一]曳長庚之飛髾，載日月之太常，棲招搖與玄弋，注枉矢於天狼。

translations.literal: [1] Trailing Chang Geng's flying streamers, bearing sun-and-moon Grand Constant, perching Zhaoyao and Black Lance, fixing Crooked Arrow on Sirius.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Streamers trail like the planet Venus; the Grand Constant bears sun-and-moon blazons; Guiding Lance and Dark Dart staffs rear beside Sirius.

sentences.zh: [二]羽毛紛其髟鼬，揚金亨而扦玉瓖。

translations.literal: [2] Feathers and plumes riot in flowing locks; they raise golden ting and brace jade xi rings.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Plumes toss like loosened hair; bells of bronze clang while jade cheek-pieces flash.

sentences.zh: [三]屯田車於平原，播同徒於高岡，旃旝摻其如林，錯五色以摛光。

translations.literal: [3] Mass field chariots on the plain; scatter retainers on high ridges; pennants and signal flags bristle like a forest; five colors interweave and shed light.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Supply wagons ring the flats, runners swarm the ridges, felt banners crowd like woods, and five-colored silks braid shifting light.

sentences.zh: [四]清氛埃，埽野場，誓六師，搜鑈良。

translations.literal: [4] Clear mist-dust, sweep the wild arena, pledge the six hosts, search out fine blades.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Haze lifts from the drill ground, heralds bind oaths on six armies, smiths test keen weapons.

sentences.zh: [五]司徒勒卒，司馬平行，車攻馬同，教達戒通。

translations.literal: [5] The Minister of Education reins the ranks; the Minister of War aligns lines; chariot assault, horses matched; instruction reaches, warnings penetrate.
translations.idiomatic: [5] The Minister of Education tightens files, the marshal aligns wings—chariots level, teams paired—so orders run clear through every command.

sentences.zh: [六]伐咎鼓，撞華鐘，獵徒縱，赴榛叢。

translations.literal: [6] Strike the fault drum, clash splendid bells; huntsmen loose, rush into bramble thickets.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Fault drums thunder, ritual bells boom, beaters spring forward into scrub oak.

sentences.zh: [七]徽嫿霍奕，別騖分奔，騷擾聿皇，往來交舛，紛紛回回，南北東西。

translations.literal: [7] Shimmering swift and flashing grand, part-striving, divide-running, restless yuhuang, coming and going crossing awry, swirling round and round, south north east west.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Teams flare and wheel—chargers split, squadrons veer, dust boiling east, west, south, north without pattern.

sentences.zh: [八]風行雲轉，匈糝隱訇，黃塵勃滃，闇若霧昏。

translations.literal: [8] Wind moves, clouds turn; chest-grain rumbles hidden; yellow dust surges up dark like fog-dusk.
translations.idiomatic: [8] Wind and cloud spin together; hooves drum like muffled thunder; ocher dust boils until noon seems twilight.

sentences.zh: [九]日月為之籠光，列宿為之翳昧，僄狡課才，勁勇程氣。

translations.literal: [9] Sun and moon for it cage light; the asterisms for it dim; darting craft tests talent; sturdy valor gauges qi.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Sun and moon dull among dust; constellations fade—swift riders prove skill, stout men measure mettle.

sentences.zh: [一０]狗馬角逐，鷹□競鷙，驍騎旁佐，輕車橫厲，相與陸梁，聿皇於中原。

translations.literal: [10] Dogs and horses vie in chase; hawks (graph missing in text) contend fiercely; fierce riders flank; light chariots cross fierce—together they rampage, yuhuang in the central plain.
translations.idiomatic: [10] Hounds and racers spur each other; hawks stoop where text fails; cataphracts sweep the flanks, light cars shear sideways—the whole host stampedes the heartland.

sentences.zh: 絹猑縞，鏦特肩，脰完羝，撝介鮮，散毛族，梏羽腢。

translations.literal: Silk fine-hounds, pale streaks; spear solo shoulders; neck-ring rams; wave-armored fresh; scatter fur clans; fetter-feather paired.
translations.idiomatic: Greyhounds in silk leashes, pale coursers, bull-necks ringed, lacquered armor gleaming, fur and feather prizes corded by teams.

sentences.zh: [一一]然後飛鋋電激，流矢雨墜，各指所質，不期俱殪，竄伏扔輪，發作梧羆。

translations.literal: [11] Then flying shuo lightning-strikes, streaming arrows rain-fall; each aims its quarry; without plan all perish; crouch-hiding toss wheels; burst forth as grotto bears.
translations.idiomatic: [11] Iron lances flash like lightning, arrows sheet down—every hunter finds his mark; quarry drops mid leap; rolling wheels flush bears from stone dens.

sentences.zh: [一二]祋殳狂擊，頭陷顱碎，獸不得猭，禽不得瞥。

translations.literal: [12] Sha-staves strike wildly; heads cave, skulls shatter; beasts cannot flee; birds cannot glance away.
translations.idiomatic: [12] Butt-spiked staffs crunch bone—no beast slips the cordon, no bird darts a sidelong escape.

sentences.zh: [一三]或夷由未殊，顛狽頓躓，蝡蝡蟫蟫，充衢塞隧，葩華□布，不可勝計。

translations.literal: [13] Some linger not yet dead; stumble fall overturn; wriggle crawl; fill roads block tunnels; splendid blooms (graph missing) scatter—beyond reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: [13] Some quarry still twitches, some sprawls headlong—beasts writhe in heaps choking paths where blossoms fleck the dust beyond count.
[14] The received text marks citation fourteen without verse—likely a lacuna in the manuscript.

translations.literal: Note [1] Yang new moon: the tenth month's new moon.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: ‘yang new moon’ is the first day of the tenth month.

translations.literal: Shu lou means carved openwork.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Openwork’ here means open-carved bronze.

sentences.zh: 周遷輿服雜記曰：「玉路，重*

translations.literal: Zhou Qian’s Mixed Record of Chariot and Robes says: "The jade road, heavy
translations.idiomatic: A Zhou ritual note begins: the jade-sheathed chariot, with heavy

translations.literal: (jiao)
translations.idiomatic: the graph jiao (uprights).

translations.literal: **[lu]* carriage."
translations.idiomatic: The gloss ends with the lu carriage-body.”

translations.literal: The golden road and jade road are the same in form.
translations.idiomatic: The gold- and jade-banded state cars share one pattern.

translations.literal: Six: it is yoked to six horses.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Six’ means a six-horse team.

translations.literal: " The Xu Han zhi says: "The Son of Heaven’s five chariots are drawn by six horses."
translations.idiomatic: The Later Han monograph says the emperor’s five chariots use six bays each.”

translations.literal: Suxian is a horse’s name.
translations.idiomatic: Suxian names a famous bay pattern.

sentences.zh: 左傳雲，唐成公有兩驌獮馬。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan states Duke Cheng of Tang had two suxian horses.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan credits Duke Cheng of Tang with a pair of suxian steeds.

translations.literal: The Zhou li says: "A horse eight chi tall is called dragon.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites measure eight chi from withers to crown as a ‘dragon’ horse.”

translations.literal: " The Record of Rites says: "In mid-winter ride the black carriage; yoke ink-black bays.
translations.idiomatic: Winter ritual rides the sable coach behind iron-black stallions.”

translations.literal: " Now this too follows winter qi and rides black.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong likewise aligns with winter’s colorway—dark carriage, dark horses.

sentences.zh: 郭璞注爾雅云：「虹雙出色鮮盛者為雄。

translations.literal: Guo Pu’s commentary on the Er ya says: "When the rainbow shows twin arcs the brighter is male.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu calls the brighter rainbow arc the male rainbow.

translations.literal: " The Zuo Zhuan says: "The dance-master inscribed the jing xia.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan has the master of dance emblazon the great standard.”

translations.literal: " Du Yu’s commentary says: "Jing xia is a great banner."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: jing xia is the king’s great ensign.”

translations.literal: Jie means to raise; pronunciation qu-lie fan.
translations.idiomatic: Jie: read qu-lie fan, ‘to lift aloft.’

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「前有塵埃，則載鳴鳶。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: "If there is dust ahead, then display the crying kite.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites says to fly the kite banner when dust swirls ahead—an omen of wind.”

translations.literal: " Yuan is the marsh harrier; pronunciation yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan is the kite; read yuan.

sentences.zh: 鳴則風動，故畫之於旌旗以候埃塵也。

translations.literal: When it cries, wind moves; therefore it is painted on flags to watch for dust.
translations.idiomatic: Kites cry before a gust, so their image on silk warned the vanguard of rising dust.

sentences.zh: 橦者，旗之竿也，音直江反。

translations.literal: Tong is the flag’s pole; pronunciation zhi-jiang fan.
translations.idiomatic: Tong is the flagstaff—fanqie zhi-jiang.

translations.literal: Note [2] Chang Geng is the Great White star.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the ‘long Geng’ star is Venus.

sentences.zh: 髾音所交反，即旌旗所垂之羽毛也。

translations.literal: Shao is read suo-jiao fan; that is, the down-hanging feathers of the standard.
translations.idiomatic: Shao (suo-jiao) names the plumes that trail from the banner’s edge.

sentences.zh: 太常，天子所建大旗也，畫之日月。

translations.literal: Grand Constant: the great flag the Son of Heaven plants, painted with sun and moon.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Constant is the royal standard blazoned with sun and moon.

translations.literal: The Zhou li says: "Sun and moon make the ‘constant.’
translations.idiomatic: The Rites of Zhou define the ‘constant’ as the sun-moon blazon.”

translations.literal: " Zhaoyao, Black Lance, and Sirius are all star names.
translations.idiomatic: Bootes’ Lance, the Dark Dart, and Sirius are asterisms painted on the vexilla.

translations.literal: Crooked Arrow: an ominous star, serpent-writhing with tail and eyes,
translations.idiomatic: The Crooked Arrow meteor trails like a snake—

translations.literal: (chi)
translations.idiomatic: text variant chi ‘red.’

translations.literal: **[also]* painted on the standards too."
translations.idiomatic: —likewise emblazoned on the army’s silks.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]髟鼬，羽旄飛揚魍也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Biao you is the appearance of plumes and yaktails flying.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: biao you depicts plumage whipping in the wind.

translations.literal: Biao is read bi-you fan.
translations.idiomatic: Biao: fanqie bi-you.

translations.literal: You is read yang-jiu fan.

translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong explains the bronze crest on the horse’s brow—four cun square—just ahead of the animal’s

translations.idiomatic: Xiang are jade cheek-straps; read xiang.

sentences.zh: 注[四]詩小雅曰：「我車既好。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Lesser Ya of the Book of Poetry says: "Our chariots are fine.
translations.idiomatic: The Lesser Ya sings: ‘Our chariots are trim.’

translations.literal: " It also says: "The archers are already matched.
translations.idiomatic: It adds: ‘Our bowmen move as one.’

translations.literal: " It speaks of the followers’ belt-hooks being aligned.
translations.idiomatic: Meaning the attendants’ toggles match rank on rank.

translations.literal: Kuai is also zhan; pronunciation gu-hui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai equals felt banners—fanqie gu-hui.

translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan drums when the commander’s kuai lifts.

translations.literal: " Shen is read suo-jin fan, same character as ‘forest-thick sen.’
translations.idiomatic: Shen (suo-jin) matches the character sen ‘dense.’

sentences.zh: 注[五]野場謂除其草萊，令得驅馳也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Wild arena means clearing its weeds and stubble so one may gallop.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: ‘wild arena’ means scythed ground fit for the chase.

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan says: "The Son of Heaven has six hosts.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan credits the Son of Heaven with six armies.

translations.literal: " Nie liang: fine horses.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Nie liang’ glosses as keen mounts.

translations.idiomatic: The Minister of Education takes marching orders from the marshal before mobilizing hunts or levies.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「司馬狩田，以旌為左右和之門。

translations.literal: " It also says: "When the Minister of War hunts, he takes standards as the gates of left and right harmony.
translations.idiomatic: The marshal marks left and right gates of the battue with paired banners.

sentences.zh: 前後有屯，百步有司，巡其前後。

translations.literal: Front and rear have encampments; every hundred paces an officer patrols front and rear.
translations.idiomatic: Van and rear camps anchor the line; officers pace every hundred paces.

translations.literal: " Zheng’s commentary says: "Rectify the warriors’ ranks.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan: straighten each file.

sentences.zh: 」詩小雅曰：「我車既攻，我馬既同。

translations.literal: " The Lesser Ya says: "Our chariots are strong; our horses are matched.
translations.idiomatic: The ode repeats: ‘Our cars are tight; our teams move as one.’

translations.idiomatic: Mao Heng glosses gong as ‘solid,’

translations.literal: Tong means aligned.
translations.idiomatic: tong as ‘aligned.’

translations.literal: In military affairs alignment of strength esteems might.
translations.idiomatic: In war you prize united muscle;

translations.literal: In hunting alignment of hooves esteems speed."
translations.idiomatic: in the hunt you prize matched hooves for speed.”

sentences.zh: 注[七]咎鼓，大鼓也，音公刀反。

translations.idiomatic: The ‘fault’ drum is the great drum—fanqie gong-dao.

translations.idiomatic: The Rites record the royal drum’s length though the first graph is lost.

sentences.zh: 注[八]嫿音呼獲反，並奔馳貌。

translations.idiomatic: Hua (fanqie huo-huo) paints horses at full gallop.

translations.idiomatic: Shen and hong gloss hoof-thunder—fanqie ku-gai and huo-hong.

translations.idiomatic: Weng: fanqie wu-dong.

translations.idiomatic: Commentary: piao jiao marks daring agility.

translations.idiomatic: Piao: fanqie pi-miao.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]絹，系也，與罥通，音工犬反。

translations.idiomatic: Kun gao names steppe ponies.

translations.idiomatic: The Er ya praises kun gao for climbing broken ridges.

translations.idiomatic: Cong means to spear or strike.

sentences.zh: 楊雄方言曰：「吳楚之閒，或謂矛為鏦。

translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong notes Wu-Chu dialects name spears cong.

translations.idiomatic: Fanqie chu-jiang.

translations.idiomatic: Han Ying’s Qi ode runs ‘pair-chasing the three-year boars.’

translations.literal: " Lord Xue’s commentary says: "A beast three years old is called shoulder.
translations.idiomatic: Xue Han: a boar in its third year is a ‘shoulder.’

translations.literal: " Dou means neck; it means striking the neck.
translations.idiomatic: Dou is the throat—here a neck shot.

translations.literal: Dou is pronounced dou.
translations.idiomatic: Dou as in ‘bean.’

translations.literal: Wan di: wild ram.
translations.idiomatic: Wan di is the wild billy-goat.

sentences.zh: 臣賢案：字書作「羦」，音戶官反，與「完」通。

translations.literal: Your servant Xian notes: the character guides write ‘yuan,’ pronunciation hu-guan fan, interchangeable with ‘wan.’
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian cites lexicons spelling it yuan (hu-guan), cognate with wan.

translations.literal: Gu: all masters read gu-ku fan.
translations.idiomatic: Gu receives gu-ku fan everywhere.

sentences.zh: 案字書「梏」從「手」，即古文「攪」字，謂攪擾也。

translations.literal: Inspecting the character guide, ‘gu’ under ‘hand’ is the ancient character ‘jiao,’ meaning to disturb.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen’s gu with hand radical is archaic ‘disturb’—not fetters.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]鋋，矛也，音巿延反。

translations.literal: Note [12] Chuan is a spear; pronunciation shi-yan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Chuan names light throwing spears—fanqie shi-yan.

sentences.zh: 周禮曰：「王弓以授射甲革、椹質者。

translations.literal: The Zhou li says: "The king’s bow is given to shoot armor, leather, and pounding-blocks.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites arm royal archers with bows fit to pierce armor and targets.

translations.literal: " Zheng’s commentary says: "Zhi means upright.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng glosses zhi as ‘true,’

translations.idiomatic: read zheng like ‘expedition.’

translations.literal: " It speaks of being crushed by wheels.
translations.idiomatic: Meaning quarry pinned under chariot wheels.

translations.idiomatic: Wu ‘prop’ reads like ‘awaken.’

translations.literal: It means bracing against the chariot.
translations.idiomatic: Beasts wedge against axles to resist.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]祋亦殳也，音丁外反。

translations.idiomatic: Dui equals the butt-spiked sha—fanqie ding-wai.

translations.literal: Lu is the forehead; pronunciation lu.
translations.idiomatic: Lu is crown or brow.

translations.idiomatic: Quan is ‘bolt’—fanqie chou-lian.

translations.idiomatic: Pie is a sidelong glance—fanqie pi-li where the book breaks.

translations.idiomatic: Sha as in ‘special.’

translations.idiomatic: Commentary: yi you is hesitation.

translations.idiomatic: The Songs of Chu sighs ‘wavering, you never march.’

translations.literal: " ‘Not yet distinct’ means not yet dead.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Not yet ended’ means still twitching.

translations.idiomatic: Ruan: fanqie ru-yan.

translations.idiomatic: Yin (si-lin) likewise paints squirming prey.

sentences.zh: 若夫鷙獸□蟲，倨牙黔口，大匈哨後，縕巡歐紆，負隅依阻，莫敢嬰御。

translations.literal: As for fierce birds and nameless insects, arrogant fangs and ink-dark muzzles, great chest and whistling hind, writhing coils and looping twists, they lean on crags—none dare brush their guard.
translations.idiomatic: When taloned birds and nameless crawlers bare black muzzles, swell chests, whistle hindquarters, and coil in defensible rocks, none dare close.

sentences.zh: [一] 乃使鄭叔、晉婦之徒，睽孤刲刺，裸裎袒裼。

translations.literal: [1] Then he sends Zheng Shu and Jin Fu’s sort—lone slayers stabbing bare, stripped to the waist.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Then professionals like Zheng Shu and the Jin huntress—solitary killers in half-clad combat.

sentences.zh: [二]冒□柘，槎棘枳，窮浚谷，底幽嶰，暴斥虎，搏狂兕，獄□熊，抾封狶。

translations.literal: [2] Breasting mulberry brakes (text lacuna), snapping jujube thorns, plumbing steep ravines, footing gloomy gorges, grappling striped tigers, wrestling mad rhinos, penning caged bears where graphs fail, seizing giant boars.
translations.idiomatic: [2] They ram mulberry brakes, tear jujube thickets, sound dark ravines, leap misted canyons—wrestling tigers, rhinos, bears where text fails, grappling giant swine.

sentences.zh: [三]或輕訬趬悍，廋疏嶁領，犯歷嵩巒，陵喬松，履修樠，踔攳枝，杪標端，尾蒼蜼，掎玄譸，木產盡，寓屬單。

translations.literal: [3] Some are light and nimble, vaulting lean ridges, crossing Song peaks, treading tall pines, mounting twisted limbs, skimming treetops, tailing gray gibbons, dragging dark macaques—wood-born prey exhausted, tree dwellers picked clean.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Lithe runners skip shale ribs, scale Song summits, swing pine branches, race gibbons through the canopy till every arboreal beast is spent.

sentences.zh: [四]罕罔合部，□弋同曲，類行並驅，星布麗屬，曹伍相保，各有分局。

translations.literal: [4] Rare nets join squads; name-lost grapplers share arcs; kinds march abreast; stars spread in glittering rows; squads shelter mates—each holds its station.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Mesh rare nets with corded squads, bend bows in concert, advance like constellations, and keep each platoon in its lane.

sentences.zh: [五] 矰碆飛流，纖羅絡縸，游雉腢驚，晨鳧輩作，翬然雲起，霅爾雹落。

translations.literal: [5] Zeng and bo fly streaming; fine nets interlink; roving pheasants call in pairs startled; morning mallards rise in flocks; whirling they cloud upward; sha-like they hail downward.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Line sinkers and whistling bolts fly; mesh nets quilt the sky—pheasants flare, teal thunder upward like driven cloud, then strikers rain like hail.
[6] The manuscript marks citation six without further verse—a lacuna in the received Guangcheng text.

sentences.zh: 注[一]爾雅曰：「駁如馬，倨牙食虎豹。

translations.idiomatic: The Er ya describes the bo steed with curling tusks that prey on big cats.

translations.idiomatic: Mencius cites Tong stalking a tiger that backs into a cliff—no one closes.

translations.idiomatic: Ying means ‘press’ or harry.
