translations.idiomatic: The Guang ya glosses sha as sudden shower.

translations.literal: " It speaks of birds struck by tether-shafts falling like hail.
translations.idiomatic: Birds struck by yi bolts drop like hailstones.

sentences.zh: 爾乃□觀高蹈，改乘回轅，泝恢方，撫馮夷，策句芒，超荒忽，出重陽，厲雲漢，橫天潢。

translations.literal: Then … viewing high treading, changing mounts and turning yoke-bar, su ford vast square, patting Ping Yi, whipping Gou Mang, leaping vast blur, exiting double yang, crossing cloudy Han, spanning celestial ford.
translations.idiomatic: Then the hunt pivots—chariots wheel, oarsmen invoke river gods, leap liminal mists, pierce heaven’s ninth layer, breast the Milky Way, ford the celestial pier.

sentences.zh: [一]導鬼區，逕神場，詔靈保，召方相，驅厲疫，走蜮祥。

translations.literal: [1] Guide ghost realms, thread spirit grounds, summon spirit guards, call Squad-Facing, drive plague demons, rout yi omens.
translations.idiomatic: [1] They chart ghost districts, pace ritual fields, conjure shamans, parade exorcists, scourge epidemics, banish river sprites.

sentences.zh: [二]捎罔兩，拂游光，枷天狗，增墳羊。

translations.literal: [2] Brush wangliang, sweep wandering lights, yoke sky-dogs, pile tomb-sheep.
translations.idiomatic: [2] They whip marsh demons, net will-o’-wisps, leash meteor dogs, heap graveyard omens.

sentences.zh: [三]然後緩節舒容，裴回安步，降集波□，川衡澤虞，矢魚陳罟。

translations.literal: [3] Then slow pace easy manner, lingering calm steps, descend to wave … (graph missing), river marsh superintendents and marsh wardens, shoot fish and spread nets.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Pageantry slows—boats beach at broken graphemes while marsh officers net fish.

sentences.zh: [四]茲飛、宿沙，田開、古蠱，[五]翬終葵，揚關斧，刊重冰，撥蟄戶，測潛鱗，踵介旅。

translations.literal: [4] Zi fei, Suosha, Tian Kai, Gu gu, [5] wield zhong kui axes, flourish frontier halberds, chip thick ice, poke hibernating dens, probe lurking scales, follow armored schools.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Legendary divers and ancient ichthyists swing axes, split ice, probe pools, trail scaled hosts.

sentences.zh: [六]逆獵湍瀨，渀薄汾橈，淪滅潭淵，左挈夔龍，右提蛟鼉，春獻王鮪，夏薦□黿。

translations.literal: [6] Hunt rushing shallows, pound bending oars, sink deep pools, left clutch Kui dragons, right drag crocodiles, spring offer king sturgeon, summer present … giant softshell (graph missing).
translations.idiomatic: [6] They strike rapids, churn oars, dredge pools, wrestle flood-dragons, drag river saurians, tabulate seasonal tribute fish.

sentences.zh: [七]於是流覽□照，殫變極態，上下究竟，山谷蕭條，原野嵺愀，上無飛鳥，下無走獸，虞人植旍，獵者□具，車弊田罷，旋入禁囿。

translations.literal: [7] Then drifting survey … shine (graph missing), exhaust every transformation, top and bottom searched out, valleys bleak, plains desolate—no bird above, no beast below; wardens plant pennons, hunters … gear (graph missing); chariots spent, hunt ends, wheel back into the forbidden park.
translations.idiomatic: [7] The hosts scour hill and dale bare—only banners and spent gear remain before re-entering the imperial preserve.

sentences.zh: [八]棲□乎昭明之觀，休息乎高光之榭，以臨乎宏池。

translations.literal: [8] Roost … at the Bright-Shining belvedere, rest on the High-Light kiosk, overlooking the great pool.
translations.idiomatic: [8] They lodge at the Bright View tower, nap on the High Balustrade, overlook the mirror lake.

sentences.zh: [九]鎮以瑤台，純以金堤，樹以蒱柳，被以綠莎，瀇瀁沆漭，錯紾盤委，天地虹洞，固無端涯，大明生東，月朔西陂。

translations.literal: [9] Firm with jade terrace, pure with golden dike, planted with cattail-willow, draped in green sedge, vast billows endless, twists and coils—Heaven and earth rainbow-gulf without bound; great brightness rises east, moon new-moons west slope.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Jade terraces and golden levees rim reed banks—waters swell boundlessly while sun climbs east and moon trims west.

sentences.zh: [一０]乃命壺涿，驅水蠱，逐罔、螭，滅短狐，簎鯨、鯢。

translations.literal: [10] Then command Hu Zhuo, drive water pests, pursue wang and chi, wipe out archer foxes, spear whales and ni?
translations.idiomatic: [10] Sorcerers scour shoals—drive flood bugs, net river bogeys, harpoon leviathans.

sentences.zh: [一一]然後方余皇，連舼舟，張雲帆，施蜺幬，靡颸風，陵迅流，發棹歌，縱水謳，淫魚出，蓍蔡浮，湘靈下，漢女游。

translations.literal: [11] Then square yuhuang, chain wing boats, spread cloud sails, hang rainbow curtains, ride slick winds, breast swift currents, raise oar songs, loose river lays—sporting fish leap, tortoise shells surface, Xiang consorts descend, Han maidens roam.
translations.idiomatic: [11] Pleasure craft unfurl silk sails on breezes—fish leap, omens rise, river goddesses drift aboard.

sentences.zh: [一二]水禽鴻鵠，鴛鴦、鷗、鷖，鶬鴰、鸕、鷁，鷺、鴈、鷿□，乃安斯寢，戢翮其涯。

translations.literal: [12] Waterfowl swans and wild geese, mandarin ducks and gulls, herons and cormorants, egrets and grebes … settle and sleep, folding wings along the shore.
translations.idiomatic: [12] Every manner of waterbird folds wings on the berm.

sentences.zh: [一三]魴、鱮、釬、□，鰋、鯉、鱨、魦，樂我純德，騰踴相隨，雖靈沼之白鳥，孟津之躍魚，方斯蔑矣。

translations.literal: [13] Bream, bighead, … (lacuna), catfish, carp, sheatfish, sand-minnows—they delight in our pure virtue, leap and follow—though Ling pond’s white birds or Meng ford’s leaping fish hardly compare.
translations.idiomatic: [13] Fish schools surge toward virtue—more vivid than Zhou omens of white birds or leaping prey.

sentences.zh: [一四]然猶詠歌於伶蕭，載陳於方策，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: [14] Yet still they are sung by flute players and filed on bamboo slips—is it not lamentable!
translations.idiomatic: [14] Yet chroniclers still hymn them—Ma Rong’s lament rings.

sentences.zh: [一五] 注[一]□，遠也，音名小反。

translations.literal: [15] Note [1] … means distant; pronunciation ming-xiao fan.
translations.idiomatic: [15] Commentary glosses ‘far gaze’ though the head graph fails.

sentences.zh: 田獵既罷，故改乘回轅也。

translations.literal: The hunt having ended, therefore they change mounts and turn the yoke-bar.
translations.idiomatic: After the battue the cortège swaps escort.

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan says: "Change the chariot yoke-bar and head north."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan idioms turning north after hunt.

translations.literal: " Su means ascend.
translations.idiomatic: Su means row upstream.

translations.literal: Hui means great.
translations.idiomatic: Hui glosses as vast.

translations.literal: Ping Yi is River Earl.
translations.idiomatic: Ping Yi is the Yellow River god.

translations.literal: Gou Mang is the god of the east.
translations.idiomatic: Gou Mang rules spring wood.

translations.literal: Huang hu means remote blur.
translations.idiomatic: Huang hu sketches misted distance.

translations.literal: Chong yang means Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Double yang’ names the empyrean.

translations.literal: Yun Han is the celestial river.
translations.idiomatic: Yun Han is the Milky Way.

translations.literal: Tian Huang is a constellation.
translations.idiomatic: The Celestial Ford asterism bridges heaven.

translations.literal: Note [2] Ling bao means spirit shaman.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: ling bao denotes chief conjuror.

sentences.zh: 楚辭九歌曰「思靈保兮賢姱」。

translations.literal: The Chu ci Nine Songs says "I long for Ling Bao the fair."
translations.idiomatic: The Nine Songs idolizes Ling Bao’s grace.

sentences.zh: 周禮：「方相氏掌執戈揚楯，帥百隸以驅疫。

translations.literal: Zhou li: "The Squad-Facing clan grasps spear and shield, leads the hundred thralls to drive plague."
translations.idiomatic: The Rites describe masked exorcists chasing epidemics.

sentences.zh: 」洪範五行傳曰：「蜮，射人，生於南越，謂之短狐。

translations.literal: " The Hong fan Five Elements commentary says: "Yi shoots men; born in Southern Yue they call it short fox."
translations.idiomatic: Esoteric texts blame southern yi sprites for shadow shots.

sentences.zh: 」詩蟲魚疏曰「一名射景，如□三足，今俗謂之水弩」也。

translations.literal: " The Poetry insects-and-fish commentary says "Also called shooting reflection; like … three legs; folk today call it water crossbow."
translations.idiomatic: Popular lore names the three-legged archer-frog.

translations.literal: Note [3] Shao (brush aside) is read suo-jiao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Shao in this sense uses fanqie suo-jiao.

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary on Zhou li says "Shao means remove."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng glosses shao as sweep away.

sentences.zh: 國語曰：「木石之怪曰夔、罔兩。

translations.literal: The Guoyu says: "Wood-and-stone monsters are called Kui and Wangliang."
translations.idiomatic: The Discourses speak of forest bogeys named Kui and Wangliang.

translations.literal: " Youguang is a spirit—eight brothers."
translations.idiomatic: Youguang spirits ride eight siblings.

translations.literal: Sky Dog is a star name.
translations.idiomatic: The Sky Dog is an asterism.

sentences.zh: 春秋元命包曰：「天狗主守財。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn Yuanming bao says: "The Sky Dog spirit guards treasure."
translations.idiomatic: Omencraft assigns the Sky Dog to ward wealth.

translations.literal: " Zeng means tether; pronunciation xi-lie fan.
translations.idiomatic: Zeng reads xi-lie fan and means ‘bind.’

translations.literal: Tomb-sheep is an earth monster; its shape is like a sheep.
translations.idiomatic: The fen-yang earth bogey looks ovine.

translations.literal: See the Kongzi jiayu.
translations.idiomatic: See the School Sayings of Confucius.

translations.literal: Note [4] Bo … is pool … (graphs missing in source).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary glosses the lacunar pool term though characters fail.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「□，在池中作室，可用棲鳥，入則捕之。

translations.literal: The Former Han pronunciation gloss says: "… builds chambers in pools where birds may roost—men enter to net them."
translations.idiomatic: The Han gloss describes sunken bird blinds in imperial pools.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「折竹以繩綿連，禁御使人不得往來」也。

translations.literal: " It also says "Split bamboo and rope them into floats—guards watch so none may pass."
translations.idiomatic: The same text ropes bamboo rafts to fence bathers out.

sentences.zh: 周禮「川衡，掌川澤之禁令。

translations.literal: Zhou li: "River marshals supervise bans on rivers and marshes.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites charge river marshals with stream regulations.

translations.literal: Marsh wardens administer state marsh edicts."
translations.idiomatic: Marsh wardens police wetland decrees.”

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「魯隱公矢魚於棠。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan says: "Duke Yin of Lu displayed fish at Tang."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Zhuan describes Duke Yin netting fish at Tang.

translations.literal: " Shi also means array.
translations.idiomatic: Here shi means ‘display.’

sentences.zh: 國語曰：「魯宣公夏濫□於泗川，裡革斷其□而□之，曰：『古者大寒降，水虞於是登川禽而嘗之於廟，行諸國助宣氣也。

translations.literal: The Guoyu says: "Duke Xuan of Lu in summer over-fished … at the Si River; Li Ge cut his … and … them, saying: ‘In antiquity when great cold descended, water wardens offered river birds for temple tasting and circulated them through the states to spread qi.
translations.idiomatic: The Discourses tell how counselor Li Ge rebuked Duke Xuan’s summer seine on the Si—citing ancient midwinter sacrifices of river fowl.

sentences.zh: 今魚方孕，又行□，貪無蓺也。

translations.literal: Now the fish are breeding, yet you still practice …—greedy beyond measure.
translations.idiomatic: Today fry fill the streams yet you drag nets—sheer greed.

translations.literal: ’ The duke said: ‘That is my fault.
translations.idiomatic: The duke admits fault.

translations.literal: ’" … is pronounced yu.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged graph sounds yu.

translations.literal: Note [5] Pronunciation ye.
translations.idiomatic: Read ye like ‘smelt.’

translations.literal: Note [6] Zi fei is the same as ci fei.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary equates zi fei with the diver title ci fei.

sentences.zh: 呂氏春秋曰：「荊人佽飛，涉江中流，兩蛟繞其船。

translations.literal: The Lüshi chunqiu says: "A man of Jing, Ci Fei, crossed the river midstream; two jiao encircled his boat."
translations.idiomatic: The Springs and Autumns of Lü Buwei tells of Ci Fei beset by twin crocodiles.

translations.literal: Ci Fei drew … and leapt into the river, stabbed the jiao and killed them.
translations.idiomatic: Ci Fei plunged blade-first and slew both beasts.

sentences.zh: 」魯連子曰：「古善漁者宿沙渠子，使漁山側，雖十宿沙子不得魚焉。

translations.literal: " The Lu Lianzi says: "The ancient master fisherman Suosha Quzi sent fishers mountain-side—though Suosha stayed ten nights they caught none.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Lun proves Suosha Quzi no dull angler—mountain pools simply lack fish.

sentences.zh: 宿沙非暗於漁道也，彼山者非魚之所生也。

translations.literal: " Suosha was not blind to fishing craft—that mountain was no habitat for fish.
translations.idiomatic: Skill mattered less than habitat.

sentences.zh: 」晏子春秋曰：「公孫捷、田開強、古冶子事景公以勇，晏子勸景公饋之二桃，曰：『計功而食之。

translations.literal: " The Yanzi chunqiu says: "Gongsun Jie, Tian Kaiqiang, and Gu Yezi served Duke Jing with valor; Yanzi urged Duke Jing to feed them two peaches, saying ‘Eat according to merit.’
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ying’s tale pits three braves against two peaches.

sentences.zh: 』公孫捷*[曰：『捷]*持楯而再搏乳虎，若捷之功，可以食桃。

translations.literal: ’ Gongsun Jie said: ‘I held shield and twice fought suckling tigers—for merit like mine I may eat a peach.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun boasts shield work against tiger cubs.

sentences.zh: 』田開強曰：『吾仗兵而御三軍者再，可以食桃。

translations.literal: ’ Tian Kaiqiang said: ‘I twice bore arms and commanded three armies—I may eat a peach.
translations.idiomatic: Tian claims martial command twice over.

sentences.zh: 』古冶子曰：『吾嘗濟河，黿銜左驂以入砥柱之流，吾逆而百步，順流九里，得黿頭，鶴躍而出，可以食桃矣。

translations.literal: ’ Gu Yezi said: ‘I once forded the river when a softshell seized the left trace-horse and dragged it into the pillar rocks; I went upstream a hundred paces, downstream nine li, took the turtle’s head, and burst out like a crane—I deserve a peach.
translations.idiomatic: Gu recounts diving the rapids to kill a river monster.

translations.literal: ’ Both men returned their peaches and slit their throats.
translations.idiomatic: Two warriors yield the peaches and fall on their swords.

sentences.zh: 古冶子曰：『二子死之，吾獨生，不仁。

translations.literal: ’ Gu Yezi said: ‘If they died and I live, I am not humane.
translations.idiomatic: Gu refuses to survive alone.

translations.literal: ’ He too slit his throat.
translations.idiomatic: He joins them in death.

translations.literal: " ‘Gu’ interchanges with ‘ye.’
translations.idiomatic: Lexicons swap gu and ye graphs.

translations.literal: Hui also means brandish.
translations.idiomatic: Hui equals ‘wave.’

translations.literal: The Guang ya says: "Zhong kui is a mallet."
translations.idiomatic: The Guang ya calls the zhong kui a hammer-head.

translations.literal: " Guan axe is an axe name.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Frontier axe’ names a halberd pattern.

translations.literal: Kan means remove.
translations.idiomatic: Kan means cut away ice.

translations.literal: Zhong is like seek.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong means trace downstream.

translations.literal: Jie means scaled creatures.
translations.idiomatic: Jie covers scaled schools.

translations.literal: Lü means ranks.
translations.idiomatic: Lü denotes cohorts.

translations.literal: Note [7] Ben is read pu-gen fan;
translations.idiomatic: Ben uses fanqie pu-gen;

translations.literal: rao is nu-jiao fan: both are appearances of entering water.
translations.idiomatic: rao uses nu-jiao—both gloss diving strokes.

translations.literal: Lun mie means sink beneath the water.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Sink extinct’ means submerged.

translations.literal: Tuo is read tuo (damaged gloss).
translations.idiomatic: The crocodile graph lacks a clear fanqie here.

sentences.zh: 鮪、鱣屬也，大者為王鮪，小者為叔鮪。

translations.literal: Wei belongs to sturgeon kind; large ones are king sturgeon, small ones uncle sturgeon.
translations.idiomatic: Sturgeon tier king versus lesser ‘uncle’ fish.

sentences.zh: 禮記「季春之月，天子始乘舟，薦鮪於寢廟。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites: "Late spring month—the Son of Heaven first boards boats and presents sturgeon at the bedchamber temple.
translations.idiomatic: Spring ritual serves sturgeon at the ancestral shrine.

sentences.zh: 季夏之月，令漁師取黿」也。

translations.literal: Late summer month—order fishers to take softshell turtles."
translations.idiomatic: Summer edicts harvest river turtles.”

sentences.zh: 注[八]流覽謂周流觀覽也。

translations.literal: Note [8] Liu lan means circuit viewing.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: liu lan is sweeping reconnaissance.

translations.literal: The Zhou li says: "Plant warden pennons to gather quarry.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites plant tally flags to collect bag limits.

translations.literal: " Zheng’s commentary says: "Plant is like set tree.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng glosses plant as ‘erect.’

sentences.zh: 田上樹旗，令獲者皆致其禽也。

translations.literal: On the hunt-field raise flags so every catcher delivers quarry.
translations.idiomatic: Field pennons gather every hunter’s bag.

translations.literal: " It also says: "When chariots halt, present quarry for the yue sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Stopped cars yield birds for the spring yue rite.

translations.literal: " Commentary says: "Chariots halted means cars stop.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Chariots halt’ simply means the chase ends.

sentences.zh: 」嵺音力救反，愀音七救反，亦蕭條魍也。

translations.literal: " Liao is read li-jiu fan; qiao is read qi-jiu fan—also appearances of bleak.
translations.idiomatic: Liao and qiao binomes paint barren wastes.

translations.literal: Note [9] Hong means great.
translations.idiomatic: Hong glosses the vast pool.

translations.literal: Note [10] Chun means border; pronunciation zhi-yin fan.
translations.idiomatic: Chun means gold-faced dike—fanqie zhi-yin.

translations.literal: Pu is also willow.
translations.idiomatic: Pu willows fringe the bank.

sentences.zh: 瀇音胡廣反，瀁音養，沆音胡朗反，漭音莽，並水魍也。

translations.literal: Wang is read hu-guang fan; yang is read yang; hang is read hu-lang fan; mang is read mang—all are water appearances.
translations.idiomatic: Four binomes chart rolling flood.

translations.literal: Cuo zhen means interweave.
translations.idiomatic: Cuo zhen braids currents.

translations.literal: Zhen is read zhi-ren fan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen: fanqie zhi-ren.

translations.literal: Wei is read yu-wei fan.
translations.idiomatic: Wei: fanqie yu-wei.

translations.literal: Hong dong means linked together.
translations.idiomatic: Hong dong means seamless horizon.

translations.literal: Hong is read hu-gong fan.
translations.idiomatic: Hong: fanqie hu-gong.

translations.literal: Shuo means birth.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo names moonrise.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「大明生於東，月生於西。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: "Great brightness is born in the east; the moon is born in the west.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites pairs eastern sun and western moon.

translations.literal: " Zheng’s commentary says: "Great brightness is the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng identifies Great Brightness as sun.

sentences.zh: 」言池水廣大，日月出於其中也。

translations.literal: " It speaks of the pool water’s vastness—the sun and moon seem born within it.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong imagines sun and moon rising from the lake.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]周禮：「壺涿氏掌除水蠱。

translations.literal: Note [11] Zhou li: "The Hu Zhuo clan drives away water pests.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites assign Hu Zhuo priests to scour flood bogeys.

translations.literal: " Zhuo is read ding-jue fan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuo: fanqie ding-jue.

translations.literal: Gu is read gong-hu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Gu: fanqie gong-hu.

translations.literal: Wang means wangliang.
translations.idiomatic: Wang equals marsh sprites.

translations.literal: Chi is dragon
translations.idiomatic: Chi names hornless dragons—

translations.literal: (ye)
translations.idiomatic: text particle ye.
—of that ilk.”

translations.literal: Short fox is yi.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Short fox’ equals the yi sprite.

translations.literal: Ce is read qi-yi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Ce: fanqie qi-yi.

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Stab."
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen glosses ce as skewering.

translations.literal: " Zhou li: "… men seasonally spear fish … turtles and clams.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites name seasonal fishers though one graph fails.

sentences.zh: 」鄭觿注云：「簎謂以杖刺泥中搏取之。」

translations.literal: " Zheng Zhong’s commentary says: "Ce means to stab mud with a pole and seize prey."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Zhong describes pole-spearing mudfish.”

translations.literal: Note [12] Fang means side by side.
translations.idiomatic: Fang marks paired hulls.

translations.literal: Yuhuang is Wu’s ship name.
translations.idiomatic: Yuhuang was the great Wu dreadnought.

translations.literal: See the Zuo Zhuan.
translations.idiomatic: See Zuo’s battle of Huangchi.

translations.literal: Qiong is a small boat; pronunciation qu-gong fan.
translations.idiomatic: Qiong skiffs use fanqie qu-gong.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰：「越舼、蜀艇，不能無水而浮。

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says: "Yue qiong and Shu boats cannot float without water.
translations.idiomatic: The Huainanzi notes even light craft need waves.

translations.literal: " Fan is pronounced fan.
translations.idiomatic: Fan as in ‘sail.’

translations.literal: Chou is canopy; pronunciation zhi-you fan.
translations.idiomatic: Chou is awning—fanqie zhi-you.

translations.literal: Si is swift wind; pronunciation chu-yi.
translations.idiomatic: Si names slick breezes.

sentences.zh: 武帝秋風詞曰：「蕭鼓鳴兮發棹歌。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu’s Autumn Wind Song says: "Zithers and drums sound—oar songs rise.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Che’s lyric opens with drums and boat songs.

sentences.zh: 」劉向列女傳曰：「津更之女，中流奏河激之歌。

translations.literal: " Liu Xiang’s Biographies of Women says: "The ferryman’s daughter sang ‘River Surge’ midstream.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang records the ferry girl’s river hymn.

sentences.zh: 」韓詩外傳曰：「瓠巴鼓琴，淫魚出聽。

translations.literal: " The Han shi waizhuan says: "When Hu Ba strummed his zither, wanton fish came to listen.

sentences.zh: [一]然後擺牲班禽，淤賜犒功，腢師疊伍，伯校千重，山罍常滿，房俎無空。

translations.literal: [1] Then they spread victims and array quarry, feast gifts and reward merit; dove commands stack squads, regional captains layer thousandfold—mountain goblets stay full, chamber stands never empty.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Victims fan out, quarry ranks, wine saturates merit—stacked cohorts, thousandfold captains, mountain urns brimming, sacrifice boards laden.”

sentences.zh: [二]酒正案隊，膳夫巡行，清醪車湊，燔炙騎將，鼓駭舉爵，鐘鳴既觴。

translations.literal: [2] Wine stewards align squads, stewards patrol lines—clear mash carts crowd in; roast riders advance; drums startle cup-lifts; bells toll till cups drain.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Intoxicants roll by cart, chefs patrol ranks—clear ale foams, skewers ride courier mounts—drums thunder with each toast.”

sentences.zh: [三]若乃陽阿衰斐之晉制，闡□華羽之南音，[四]所以洞蕩匈臆，發明耳目，疏越蘊慉，駭恫底伏，[五]鍠鍠鎗鎗，奏於農郊大路之衢，與百姓樂之。

translations.literal: [3] Then Yang'e decay-feather Jin modes and chan … garbed southern tones [4] thereby cleanse breast and belly, open ears and eyes, scatter hoarded cares, shake dormant dread [5] clang clang clash clash—they play at the farm suburb great crossing’s junction for the hundred clans to rejoice.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Jin's Yang'e airs and lacunar southern dances purge the breast, wake ear and eye, scatter pent cares—bells and drums clang along the highroad where commons throng.”

sentences.zh: [六]是以明德曜乎中夏，威靈暢乎四荒，東鄰浮巨海而入享，西旅越□領而來王，南徼因九譯而致貢，朔狄屬象胥而來同。

translations.literal: [6] Therefore bright virtue shines within the Middle Kingdom; awesome numen flows to the four wastes—east neighbors float the great sea to join sacrifice; western hosts cross … ridges to declare kingship; southern marches relay nine interpreters for tribute; northern Di attach to interpreters and seek harmony.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Virtue radiates inward while spirits reach beyond seas—eastern islands sail to sacrifice, western tribes ford unnamed ridges, southern tongues chain nine relays, northern herds attach translators.”

sentences.zh: [七]蓋安不忘危，治不忘亂，道在乎茲，斯固帝王之所以曜神武而折遐沖者也。

translations.literal: [7] To rest yet recall peril, rule yet recall chaos—the Way lies herein; thus indeed emperors display divine martial prowess and break distant thrusts.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Peace remembers danger—such is the Way kings wield spirit arms and blunt distant blades.”

translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong contrasts Han moral triumph with neglected martial drills.”

sentences.zh: [一] 闇昧不鶯日月之光，聾昏不聞雷霆之震，於今十二年，為日久矣。

translations.literal: [1] Dimmed eyes catch no sun or moon; deaf ears hear no thunder—twelve years now—long indeed.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Twelve years without royal hunts blind and deafen the realm to Heaven’s majesty.”

sentences.zh: 亦方將刊禁台之秘藏，發天府之官常，由質要之故業，率典刑之舊章。

translations.literal: Also now about to publish the forbidden terrace's secret treasuries, open the celestial treasury's official norms, follow bond-contract inherited enterprises, uphold statute-pattern old statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Next the king would unlock palace archives, harmonize statutes with ancient deeds, and revive exemplary law.

sentences.zh: [二]采清原，嘉岐陽，登俊桀，命賢良，舉淹滯，拔幽荒。

translations.literal: [2] Select Qingyuan, praise Qiyang, elevate towering talents, commission worthy and good, promote the long submerged, raise men from remote wastes.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He would tour Qingyuan’s precedent, honor Qi-yang’s hunt, lift genius from neglect.

sentences.zh: [三]察淫侈之華譽，顧介特之實功，聘畎畝之腢雅，宗重淵之潛龍。

translations.literal: [3] Inspect licentious extravagant hollow fame, heed solitary singular real merit, summon rustic singers from furrows, honor deep-pool hidden dragons.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Screen flashy reputations, prize solitary merit, call poets from the plow and sages from deep water.

sentences.zh: [四]乃儲精山藪，歷思河澤，目□鼎俎，耳聽康衢，營傅說於胥靡，求伊尹於庖廚，索膠鬲於魚鹽，聽□戚於大車。

translations.literal: [4] Then store refined insight among hills and marshes, travel thoughtful surveys across rivers and lakes—eyes fixed on tripod stands, ears attuned to Kang Qu—seek Fu Yue among bondsmen, Yi Yin from kitchens, Jiao Ge from fish and salt, hear … Qi from heavy carts (graphs damaged).
translations.idiomatic: [4] Like kings who fetched Yi Yin and Fu Yue—Ma Rong lists every motif though lacunas bite.

sentences.zh: [五]俾之昌言而宏議，軼越三家，馳騁五帝，悉覽休祥，總括腢瑞。

translations.literal: [5] Make them voice splendid counsels and grand deliberations, surpass the Three Archives, race the Five Thearchs, exhaust auspicious omens, gather every felicity.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Court poets would outshine the classics and tally every omen.

sentences.zh: [六]遂棲鳳皇於高梧，宿麒麟於西園，納僬僥之珍羽，受王母之白環。

translations.literal: [6] Then perch phoenix on tall paulownia, lodge qilin in the western park, admit Jiaoyao's peerless plumes, receive the Queen Mother’s white jade ring.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Omens pile—phoenix, qilin, pygmies’ feathers, Queen Mother’s ring.

sentences.zh: [七]永逍搖乎宇內，與二儀乎無疆，貳造化於后土，參神施於昊干，超特達而無儔，煥巍巍而無原。

translations.literal: [7] Forever roam idle within the cosmos, match the Two Patterns without bound, twin Creation with Hou Tu, pair divine bounty with bright Heaven—peerless ascent, blazing loftiness without origin.
translations.idiomatic: [7] The Son of Heaven would harmonize heaven and earth without rival.

sentences.zh: [八] 豐千億之子孫，歷萬載而永延。

translations.literal: [8] Multiply billions of heirs, extend myriad kalpas forever.
translations.idiomatic: [8] Bless lineage beyond counting and years without end.

sentences.zh: 一九五四頁三行 拜為校書郎中 「校」原作「挍」，逕據汲本、殿本改。

translations.idiomatic: Variant 嬉/欣 noted.

sentences.zh: 一九五五頁八行 有才不能用 按：刊誤謂「才」當作「財」。

sentences.zh: 按：集解引錢大昕說，謂「囿」當從閩本作「圃」。

translations.literal: Note: Qian Daxin argues 囿 should follow Min edition as 圃.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin favors 圃.

sentences.zh: 詩「東有甫草」，鄭氏讀如「圃」。

translations.literal: Ode ‘east has fu grass’—Zheng reads fu like pu.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan’s phonetic gloss cited.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一〇行 恢胎曠蕩 按：「恢」原作「□」，俗體字，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1956 line 10: 恢胎曠蕩—note: 恢 was vulgar □—normalized.
translations.idiomatic: Vulgar variant fixed to 恢.

translations.literal: Below ‘camp perimeter vast’ likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Same fix downstream.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一〇行 寥豁鬱泱 按：「寥」原斗「寒」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1956 line 10: 寥豁鬱泱—note: 寥 misprinted 寒; corrected per Ji/Palace.
translations.idiomatic: 寥 restored from 寒 error.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一一行 左概嵩獄 按：王念孫讀書雜誌余編謂「概」當作「枕」，字之誤也。

translations.literal: Page 1956 line 11: left embracing Song peaks—note: Wang Niansun reads 概 as 枕 error.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Niansun proposes 枕 for 概.

sentences.zh: 水經汝水注、太平御覽地部引此，並作「左枕嵩岳」。

translations.literal: The Shui jing commentary on the Ru River and the Taiping yulan geography section both quote this passage as ‘the left side pillows the Song peaks.’
translations.idiomatic: Other sources agree on the verb ‘pillow’ for the range to the left.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一一行 箕背王屋 按：王念孫謂「箕背」當作「背箕」，與「面據」相對，箕讀為基，基亦據也，言前據衡陰，後據王屋也。

translations.literal: Page 1956, line 11: ‘Ji backs Mount Wangwu’—note: Wang Niansun argues the phrase should read ‘back toward Ji,’ parallel to ‘face the north of Heng’; he reads ji as homophone for ‘base, occupy,’ so the line means the forecourt rests on the north of Mount Heng and the rear base rests on Mount Wangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Niansun reorders the line so that ‘backing Ji’ pairs with ‘facing Heng’ and explains the place-name as a pun on ‘base’ and ‘perch upon.’

sentences.zh: 水經汝水注引此，正作「背基王屋」。

translations.literal: Shui jing Ru citation reads ‘backing foundation Wangwu.’
translations.idiomatic: Ru river commentary confirms reorder.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一三行 昌本深蒱 殿本「蒱」作「蒲」，注同。

translations.literal: Page 1956 line 13: 昌本深蒱—Palace writes 蒲 for 蒱—gloss same.
translations.idiomatic: Palace uses 蒲 variant.

translations.literal: Note: 蒱 alternates with pu reed.
translations.idiomatic: Plants interchangeable.

sentences.zh: 一九五六頁一五行 豐彤對蔚 按：「彤」原作「肜」，逕依汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: Page 1956 line 15: 豐彤對蔚—note: 彤 miswritten 肜; fixed per Ji/Palace.
translations.idiomatic: 彤 corrected from 肜.

sentences.zh: 一九五七頁一五行 東曰衡山多青*

translations.literal: Page 1957 line 15: east called Heng many green*
translations.idiomatic: Shanhai jing citation stub.

translations.idiomatic: variant ju.

translations.idiomatic: Lacuna filled per classic.

translations.idiomatic: Parallel pigment gloss.

sentences.zh: 山海經凡言「青□」，皆□青作「□」，茲據改。

translations.literal: Shanhai jing wherever says ‘green □’ writes □ azure as □—emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Editor harmonizes color graphs.

sentences.zh: 一九五七頁一七行 應劭 按：「劭」原斗「邵」，逕改正。

translations.idiomatic: Qiu spring issued from a hollow—corrected against the Erya gloss.

sentences.zh: 一九五八頁一三行 爾雅曰茆鳧葵 按：「爾雅」當作「廣雅」。

translations.literal: Page 1958 line 13: Er ya says mao mallard—note: should read Guang ya.

translations.idiomatic: Tang regiment named for asterism.

translations.idiomatic: Phonology aligns Shuowen.

translations.literal: Yet ‘alternate zi-gong fan’ shows Tang-era miswriting 亨 confusing commentators.
translations.idiomatic: Later misreads spawned alternate fanqie.

sentences.zh: 一九六〇頁九行 狗馬角逐 按：汲本「角」作「爭」。

translations.literal: Page 1960 line 9: dogs horses chase contest—note: Ji reads 爭 for 角.
translations.idiomatic: Ji prefers 爭.

sentences.zh: 一九六〇頁一一行 祋殳狂擊 按：「祋」原斗「□」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1960 line 11: butt-shafts mad strike—note: 祋 misprinted □; corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Restores 祋 graph.

translations.literal: Commentary same.
translations.idiomatic: Gloss aligned.

translations.idiomatic: Comet gloss lemma.

translations.literal: The marginal gloss marks the color graph chi 'red.'
translations.idiomatic: chi variant.

sentences.zh: **[亦]*畫於旌旗也 按：刊誤謂妖星但見尾目而已，又言其赤，非也。

translations.literal: **亦 painted standards—note: Kanwu says ominous star shows tail only—adding red wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu deletes rogue chi clause.

sentences.zh: 當作「蛇行有尾目，亦畫於旌旗也」。

translations.literal: Should read ‘snakes tail eyes—also painted standards.’
translations.idiomatic: Syntax adjusted.

sentences.zh: 上文太常畫日月，故云「亦畫」也。

translations.literal: Above Grand Constant paints sun-moon—therefore ‘also paint.’
translations.idiomatic: Parallel with solar banners.

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Editors adopt revision.

sentences.zh: 一九六一頁六行 高廣各四寸在馬□前 按：續書輿服志注引獨斷，「四寸」作「五寸」，「馬□」作「馬髦」。

translations.literal: Page 1961, line 6: height and width each four cun in front of the horse□—note: the Xu Han carriage commentary quoting Duguan reads five cun instead of four cun and writes horse mane where this edition breaks.
translations.idiomatic: Later carriage manuals disagree with this line on whether the plaque measures four or five cun and on whether the ornament is general horse hair or a named tuft.

sentences.zh: 一九六一頁八行 我車既好 刊誤謂「我」當作「田」。

translations.literal: Page 1961 line 8: our chariots fine—Kanwu says 我 should be 田.
translations.idiomatic: Ode lemma 我→田 proposed.

translations.literal: Note: The Mao version of the Lesser Ode “Carriage Hunt” (Che gong) reads the graph as “field” (tian) here.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Odes’ “Carriage Hunt” line uses the word “field” at this place.

sentences.zh: 一九六二頁一行 猑縞趼 殿本「猑」作「騉」。

translations.literal: Page 1962, line 1: ji-gray calluses—the Palace edition writes the graph ji as kun (dapple-gray horse).
translations.idiomatic: On page 1962, line 1, the Palace woodblock substitutes kun for ji in the phrase about gray hides and calluses.

translations.literal: Note: The received Erya uses “kun” at this point.
translations.idiomatic: The standard Erya text likewise reads kun.

sentences.zh: 一九六二頁一一行 獄□態 按：集解引錢大昕說，謂「□」當作「猘」。

translations.literal: Page 1962, line 11: prison□ bearing—note: the collected commentary cites Qian Daxin, who says the lacuna should be read as zhi (fierce).
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin, cited in the commentary, fills the broken graph with zhi to restore the line about a prison-maddened creature.

sentences.zh: 一九六二頁一二行 杪標端 按：「標」原斗「摽」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1962, line 12: treetop marker tip—note: biao was misprinted as piao; corrected straightforwardly.
translations.idiomatic: The compositor had swapped biao for piao; the tree-crown line is emended without further comment.

translations.literal: The subcommentary follows the same correction.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss adopts the same fix.

sentences.zh: 一九六三頁四行 裎*

translations.literal: Page 1963, line 4: the lemma cheng is marked with an asterisk.
translations.idiomatic: Line 4 of page 1963 marks the lemma cheng for glossing.

translations.literal: The marginal gloss identifies the reading as tan ‘strip to the waist.’
translations.idiomatic: The gloss glosses the graph as tan, meaning bare the torso.

translations.literal: Text restored as **[luo]* ye—following the Ji and Palace woodblocks.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Palace editions read “naked,” which replaces the older wording here.

sentences.zh: 一九六四頁一行 導鬼區 按：刊誤謂「導」當作「道」。

translations.literal: Page 1964, line 1: ‘lead the ghost district’—note: Kanwu says dao ‘lead’ should be dao ‘way, district.’
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu repoints the verb to the homophone meaning track or boundary for the ghost precinct.

sentences.zh: 一九六四頁七行 樹以蒱柳 汲本、殿本「蒱」作「蒲」，注同。

translations.literal: Page 1964, line 7: planting pu willows—the Ji and Palace editions write cat-tail pu instead of rattan pu; the gloss agrees.
translations.idiomatic: Both woodblocks prefer the cat-tail character for the willow grove; the commentary matches.

translations.literal: Note: The two pu graphs are interchangeable in this context.
translations.idiomatic: Editors treat the two spellings as graphic variants here.

sentences.zh: 一九六四頁一二行 詠歌於伶蕭 按：汲本「蕭」作「簫」。

translations.literal: Page 1964, line 12: chanting songs to the musicians’ xiao—note: the Ji edition writes reed-pipe xiao instead of plant-name xiao.
translations.idiomatic: The Ji text swaps the homophone so the line refers to panpipes, not sedge.

sentences.zh: 一九六四頁一五行 帥百隸以驅疫 按：「驅」原斗「歐」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1964, line 15: leading the hundred bondsmen to expel plague—note: qu ‘drive’ was misprinted as ou; corrected straightforwardly.
translations.idiomatic: A misprint turned expel into strike; the purge-of-pestilence line is restored.

sentences.zh: 一九六五頁一一行 公孫捷*[曰捷]*持楯而再搏乳虎 據汲本補。

translations.literal: Page 1965, line 11: Gongsun Jie*[yue jie]* holding a shield and again wrestling a suckling tiger—supplied per the Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: The Ji block inserts the phrase “said Jie” into Gongsun Jie’s boast about the tiger.

sentences.zh: 按：宋本注無「曰捷」二字，故劉攽刊誤謂如下文，則此少「曰吾」二字。

translations.literal: Note: Song-edition glosses omit the two graphs “said Jie,” so Liu Ban’s Kanwu argues that, if the following lines apply, this clause lacks “said I.”
translations.idiomatic: Because early glosses lack “said Jie,” Liu Ban thinks the line should mirror the sons’ “I said” formula.

sentences.zh: 此「曰捷」二字疑毛子晉以意補之。

translations.literal: These two graphs “said Jie” were probably supplied by Mao Zijin on his own judgment.
translations.idiomatic: The extra “said Jie” looks like Mao Zijin’s conjectural patch.

sentences.zh: 張森楷校勘記謂據下二子皆曰「吾」，不自稱名，則捷亦不宜獨自稱名，劉謂少「曰吾」二字是也，未知子晉何從改作「捷」。

translations.literal: Zhang Senkai’s collation argues that because the two sons below both say “I” and never style themselves by name, Jie likewise ought not stand alone with his name; Liu’s claim that “said I” is missing is sound, but it is unclear why Mao Zijin altered it to “Jie.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Senkai sides with Liu about the missing “I said,” yet wonders why Mao substituted Jie’s given name.

sentences.zh: 一九六六頁七行 螭龍*

translations.literal: Page 1966, line 7: hornless dragon*
translations.idiomatic: Page 1966, line 7 marks the chi-dragon lemma for commentary.

translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies the particle ye.

translations.literal: Text emended to **[shu]*—following the Ji edition.

sentences.zh: 一九六六頁一〇行 蕭鼓鳴兮 按：汲本「蕭」作「簫」。

translations.idiomatic: The Ji text uses the musical instrument character in the line about pipes and drums.

sentences.zh: 一九六六頁一二行 漢水之神*[女]* 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1966, line 12: goddess of the Han River*[nü]*—nü supplied per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Palace editions add nü ‘woman’ to identify the river goddess.

translations.literal: Page 1966, line 14: gull, white gull—note: the Ji edition reads ‘white gull’ as ‘white xian pheasant.’
translations.idiomatic: The Ji block substitutes the pheasant name for the second ‘gull’ in the gloss.

translations.literal: Page 1966, line 16: an asterisk marks an editorial note on the lemma.
translations.idiomatic: Line 16 on page 1966 carries a starred lemma awaiting gloss.

translations.idiomatic: The gloss adds the adjective ‘white.’

translations.literal: Text restored as **[ye]* wild duck—following Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Palace editions read ‘wild’ before ‘duck’ in the identification.

translations.literal: Particle **[ye]* restored—following Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Palace editions reinstate the copula ye at the end of the gloss.

sentences.zh: 一九六七頁一五行 擺亦捭字也 按：「捭」原斗「裨」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1967, line 15: bai is also the graph bo—note: bo was misprinted as bi ‘minor’; corrected straightforwardly.
translations.idiomatic: A slip confused the verb ‘spread’ with ‘subsidiary’; the wordplay on bo is restored.

sentences.zh: 一九六七頁一五行 班固西都賦曰置互擺牲 按：沉欽韓謂此張衡西京賦語，注誤以為班固。

translations.literal: Page 1967, line 15: Ban Gu’s Western Capital Rhapsody says ‘set up crossing bai victims’—note: Shen Qinhan says this line belongs to Zhang Heng’s Western Metropolis Rhapsody; the commentary wrongly ascribes it to Ban Gu.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qinhan reassigns the cross-slaughter couplet to Zhang Heng and faults the attribution to Ban Gu.

sentences.zh: 一九六八頁八行 孟子謂齊*[宣]*王曰 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1968, line 8: Mencius addressed King Xuan of Qi—Xuan supplied per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Palace editions insert King Xuan’s temple name into Mencius’ address.

sentences.zh: 一九六八頁一四行 是以名通言語之官為象胥 刊誤謂「名通」當作「通名」，謂總稱言語之官為象胥也。

translations.literal: Page 1968, line 14: ‘Therefore they style the speech liaisons xiangxu’—Kanwu says ming tong should be tong ming, meaning they collectively title the language officers xiangxu.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu transposes the compound to ‘general title’ so the sentence defines the generic office name.

sentences.zh: 按：周禮鄭注作「是因通言語之官為象胥雲」，阮元校勘記謂大字本「因」下有「名」字，則刊誤之說非也。

translations.literal: Note: Zheng Xuan’s Zhou li commentary reads ‘Therefore because they connect speech the officers are called xiangxu, yun’—Ruan Yuan’s collation says the large-script edition has ming after yin, so Kanwu’s argument fails.
translations.idiomatic: Ruan Yuan shows Zheng’s text already contains ming, refuting Kanwu’s reordering.

sentences.zh: 一九六九頁三行 嘉岐陽 按：「岐」原作「歧」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1969, line 3: praising Qiyang—note: Qi mountain was miswritten as qi ‘fork’; corrected straightforwardly.
translations.idiomatic: The place-name graph is restored from a miswritten fork character.

translations.literal: The subcommentary follows the same correction.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss matches that emendation.

sentences.zh: 一九七〇頁一五行 時左將奏融*

translations.literal: Page 1970, line 15: when the Left General submitted a memorial mentioning Rong*
translations.idiomatic: The lemma on page 1970 concerns the Left General’s memorial on Ma Rong.

translations.literal: The marginal gloss records an alternate reading dao (homophone with Rong in some traditions).
translations.idiomatic: The gloss notes a dao variant beside the name Rong.

translations.literal: Text emended to **[zao]* ‘suffered’ his nephew’s bereavement—following the Palace edition.
translations.idiomatic: The Palace block reads zao ‘to suffer loss,’ yielding Ma Rong mourning a nephew.

sentences.zh: 一九七一頁一行 出為河閒王廄長史 按：刊誤謂廄長即是官名，「史」字衍。

translations.literal: Page 1971, line 1: went out as stable chief secretary to the Prince of Hejian—note: Kanwu says stable chief is already the title and the graph shi ‘scribe’ is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu deletes secretary because chief of the royal stable is a complete office by itself.
